prior_rationale: Rare term 'מֹרִיָּה' (Moriah, freq 2) is central in both 'Abraham Isaac' and 'Voice From Heaven Beloved Son'; strong thematic resonance (creation, son).

from.title: Abraham Isaac
from.body: # Abraham / Isaac → The Crucifixion ## OT Story — Genesis 22 God commands Abraham: *"Take your son, your only son, whom you love — Isaac — and go to the region of Moriah. Sacrifice him there as a burnt offering on a mountain I will show you."* (Gen 22:2) Abraham obeys. Isaac carries the wood for his own sacrifice up the mountain. Isaac asks, *"Father, where is the lamb?"* Abraham answers, *"God himself will provide the lamb."* (Gen 22:7-8) At the moment of the knife's descent, God stops Abraham and provides a ram caught in a thicket of thorns. The ram dies in Isaac's place. Abraham names the place *YHWH-Yireh* — "the LORD will provide." ## NT Fulfillment - **John 3:16** — *"For God so loved the world that he gave his only Son..."* — the verbal echo of Genesis 22:2 is exact. - **Romans 8:32** — *"He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all..."* — Paul deliberately echoes Genesis 22:12 ("you have not withheld your son"). - **Hebrews 11:17-19** — interprets the event explicitly as a typology of resurrection: Abraham received Isaac back *"figuratively speaking"* from death. - **John 1:29** — *"Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world"* — John the Baptist invokes the *seh* (שֶׂה) that Abraham promised God would provide. ## Shared structure - Father offers his **`[[../Symbols/Only-Son]]`** — verbatim Hebrew echo into Greek (*monogenēs*) - On a **mountain in the Moriah range** — traditionally identified with the Temple Mount and Calvary - Son carrie

to.title: Voice From Heaven Beloved Son
to.body: # Voice from Heaven: Beloved Son ## NT Text — Matt 3:17; 17:5 At baptism and transfiguration the Father declares, 'This is my beloved Son.' The formula fuses Psalm 2's royal coronation with Isaiah 42's Servant and possibly Genesis 22's 'only/beloved son.' ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 2:7** — Royal son begotten today. - **Isa 42:1** — Chosen in whom my soul delights. - **Gen 22:2** — Take your son, your only/beloved son. ## Shared structure - Father identifies Son - Public declaration - Mission framing - Echo of Aqedah's 'only son' ## Theological motif Three OT streams converge: Davidic king, Isaianic Servant, Abrahamic only-son. The cross is already embedded in the inaugural declaration. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is the Aqedah echo real?** *LXX uses 'agapetos' for Isaac in Gen 22; the same word here is the bridge.* 2. **Could this be a later Christological gloss?** *All four Gospels carry the declaration in some form.* ## Embedding hint A heavenly Sonship declaration fusing Psalm 2, Isaiah 42, and Genesis 22.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'θριαμβεύω' (leading in triumph) appears in both 'Ark Captured And Returned' and 'Samson Solo Victory In Death' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Ark Captured And Returned
from.body: # Ark Captured and Returned → Christ Captured, Yet Triumphant ## OT Story — 1 Samuel 4-6 Philistines capture the ark. In their temple, Dagon falls before it and is shattered. Plagues afflict their cities. They send the ark back on a new cart. ## NT Fulfillment - Ephesians 4:8: When he ascended, he led captivity captive. - Colossians 2:15: He disarmed the powers, triumphing over them. - Psalm 68:18: You ascended on high, leading a host of captives. ## Shared structure - Glory of God in apparent captivity. - Idol-god shattered before the LORD. - Triumph over enemies in their own house. - Return in glory. ## Theological motif Captivity-and-triumph of the divine presence. ## Counterarguments 1. **Ark is not Christ.** *The presence pattern (presence captured, returns victoriously) is canonically Christ-shaped.* 2. **Plagues are circumstantial.** *The narrative pointedly displays the powerlessness of rival gods.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about the presence captured yet victorious, idols falling, or triumph over rival powers.

to.title: Samson Solo Victory In Death
to.body: # Samson's Death Brings Greater Victory → Christ Triumphs by Dying ## OT Story — Judges 16:23-31 Blinded, mocked, between two pillars, Samson prays once more. He pushes; the temple collapses; he kills more in his death than in his life. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 2:14-15: Through death he destroyed the one who has the power of death. - Colossians 2:15: He disarmed the powers, triumphing over them in him. - John 12:24: Unless a grain dies, it remains alone. ## Shared structure - Stretched between pillars — cruciform posture. - Mocked enemies destroyed at the moment of apparent defeat. - Greater victory through death than life. - Self-offering after final prayer. ## Theological motif Triumph through death. ## Counterarguments 1. **Samson is morally compromised.** *The typology is structural — the shape of victory-through-death — not character imitation.* 2. **This trivializes the cross.** *Hebrews and patristic readings willingly draw the parallel.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about cruciform victory, mockers destroyed at moment of death, or final prayer of the dying deliverer.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'of zion' (freq 7) is central to both; theme overlap: kingship, mountain.

from.title: Revelation 14 Lamb On Zion
from.body: # Revelation 14: Lamb on Mount Zion ## NT Text — Rev 14:1-5 The Lamb stands on Mount Zion with 144,000 bearing his Father's name on their foreheads. Psalm 2's enthronement on Zion fulfilled. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 2:6** — I have set my king on Zion, my holy hill. - **Joel 2:32** — Mount Zion shall be refuge. - **Obad 17** — On Mount Zion there shall be deliverance. ## Shared structure - Lamb enthroned - Mount Zion - Sealed remnant - New song - Firstfruits ## Theological motif Zion fulfilled in the Lamb-king and his redeemed; counter-Zion to Babylon. ## Counterarguments 1. **Earthly Zion?** *Heavenly antitype of which earthly is shadow.* 2. **Celibate elites?** *'Virginal' is symbolic purity, not literal celibacy.* ## Embedding hint A Lamb-on-Zion enthronement of the sealed firstfruits-multitude.

to.title: Triumphal Entry On Donkey
to.body: # Triumphal Entry on a Donkey ## The oracle Zion's king comes to her, righteous and having salvation, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt the foal of a donkey, proclaiming peace to the nations. ## Date / context Zechariah, post-exilic Jerusalem, late 6th c. BC. ## Fulfillment Jesus enters Jerusalem on Palm Sunday riding a donkey's colt; the evangelists cite Zechariah explicitly (Matt 21:5; John 12:15). ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Places/Mount-Zion]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint The humble-king image — peaceable royal advent, not war-horse, but a donkey.

prior_rationale: Solid echo via '[the] propitiation' (ἱλασμός); seed and seed share coherent theological vocabulary.

from.title: 1 John 2 Atoning Sacrifice
from.body: # 1 John 2: Atoning Sacrifice ## NT Text — 1 John 2:1-2 If anyone sins we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins — and for the whole world. ## OT Antecedents - **Lev 16** — Day of Atonement propitiation. - **Isa 53:10** — Soul an offering for sin. - **Ps 32:1-2** — Blessed is he whose sin is covered. ## Shared structure - Advocate - Propitiation - Universal scope - Sin-cleansing ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice as ongoing intercession-base; mercy-seat applied. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universal atonement?** *Sufficient for all; effectual for believers.* 2. **Advocacy vs. once-for-all?** *Sacrifice once; intercession ongoing.* ## Embedding hint A propitiation-and-advocate scheme rooting Christian assurance in Christ's atoning blood.

to.title: 1 John 4 God Is Love
to.body: # 1 John 4: God Is Love ## NT Text — 1 John 4:7-12 Love is from God; whoever loves has been born of God. In this the love of God was manifested: he sent his only Son as propitiation for our sins. ## OT Antecedents - **Deut 7:7-8** — God's electing love. - **Hos 11:1-4** — Love drawing Israel. - **Ps 36:5-7** — Steadfast love of the LORD. ## Shared structure - God-as-love - Love manifested in sending - Only-Son sent - Propitiation - Love-among-saints ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice as the defining manifestation of divine love. ## Counterarguments 1. **Sentimentalism?** *Love is sacrificial and propitiatory, not mere feeling.* 2. **Universal definition of love?** *Love defined by cross, not vice versa.* ## Embedding hint A love-defined-by-cross definition citing the only-Son sent as propitiation.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'of noah' (freq 8) is central to both; theme overlap: creation_new, judgment.

from.title: Flood Baptism
from.body: # Flood / Ark → Baptism Into Christ ## OT Story — Genesis 6-9 A righteous remnant is preserved in a wooden vessel as God's judgment washes the world clean. Noah's family passes through the waters of death and emerges into a new creation, greeted by a covenant rainbow. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Peter 3:20-21: "Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you" — through-water salvation in Christ's resurrection. - Matthew 24:37-39: The days of Noah typify unwatchful generations before the Son of Man's coming. - 2 Peter 2:5: Noah, "a herald of righteousness," prefigures the gospel preached before judgment. ## Shared structure - Judgment-bearing waters pass over a covenant household. - Wooden vessel as means of rescue (cf. cross, ark of Moses, ark of covenant). - New-creation emergence: dove, olive branch, altar. - Eight souls (1 Pet 3:20) — number of new beginning. ## Theological motif Death-And-Resurrection through water; salvation through judgment, not around it. ## Counterarguments 1. **Baptism is symbolic; the flood was judgment, not salvation.** *Peter's point is precisely that the waters that judged the world saved the family inside the ark — the same dynamic in baptism united to Christ.* 2. **Typology requires authorial intent.** *1 Peter is itself canonical authorial intent, sealing the linkage.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about passing through water unto salvation, judgment on the wicked alongside rescue of a remnant, or new-creation emergence after deluge.

to.title: Noahic Covenant
to.body: # Noahic Covenant ## What it is God's post-flood covenant with Noah and all flesh, pledging never again to destroy the earth by water and reaffirming the creation mandate under new conditions of human violence and animal fear. ## Key elements - Universal scope — all flesh, every living creature - Rainbow as covenant sign - Sanctity of human blood / image of God - Renewed mandate to be fruitful and multiply - Stability of seasons guaranteed ## NT fulfillment Peter sees the flood as a type of baptism and the present world's preservation as God's patient delay before final judgment by fire (2 Pet 3; 1 Pet 3:20-21). Jesus uses Noah's days as a pattern for the unexpected arrival of the Son of Man (Matt 24:37-39). ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Noah]]`, `[[../Covenants/Edenic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Motifs/Death-And-Resurrection]]`, `[[../Numbers/Forty]]` ## Embedding hint The only universal covenant with all flesh — uniquely cosmic in scope and grounded in a meteorological sign rather than a sacrificial mark on the body.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ὀστέον' (of bones) appears in both 'Bones Not Broken' and 'Bones Of Joseph Carried Out' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Bones Not Broken
from.body: # Bones Not Broken ## NT Text — John 19:33-36 Jesus' legs are not broken; John cites the Passover-lamb statute. The true Passover Lamb dies in conformity to the Exodus rite. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 12:46** — Not a bone shall be broken. - **Num 9:12** — Same rule for Passover. - **Ps 34:20** — He keeps all his bones. ## Shared structure - Unbroken bones - Passover statute - Preservation amid death - Fulfillment ## Theological motif Lamb-of-God dies whole; Passover sealed in the corpse. ## Counterarguments 1. **Timing providential?** *Yes.* 2. **Ps 34 also?** *John may layer both.* ## Embedding hint Unbroken-bones detail fulfilling Passover-lamb statute.

to.title: Bones Of Joseph Carried Out
to.body: # Bones of Joseph Carried Out → Hope of Resurrection ## OT Story — Genesis 50:24-26 Dying in Egypt, Joseph commands: 'God will surely visit you; carry up my bones from here.' Moses takes the bones at the exodus; Joshua buries them in the land. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 11:22: By faith Joseph spoke of the exodus and gave instructions concerning his bones. - Ezekiel 37: Dry bones live by the Spirit. - 1 Corinthians 15:20: Christ the firstfruits of those who sleep. ## Shared structure - Faith in promised land beyond death. - Bones preserved for resurrection-day return. - Joseph's exit anticipates Israel's exodus and a greater bodily future. - The covenant binds the dead to the living promise. ## Theological motif Hope of resurrection embedded in patriarchal faith. ## Counterarguments 1. **Bone-burial is just custom.** *Hebrews 11:22 frames it explicitly as faith in God's future visitation.* 2. **This is not resurrection per se.** *The seed of bodily hope in the land matures into resurrection theology.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about bones preserved, faith in future visitation, or burial in the land of promise.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ὁμοιότης' (the same way) links 'Covenant Of Levi' and 'High Priest' through covenant, sacrifice.

from.title: Covenant Of Levi
from.body: # Covenant of Levi ## What it is God's covenant with the tribe of Levi for life and peace, charging the priests to fear God, give true instruction, and guard knowledge, with Malachi indicting their corruption of this covenant. ## Key elements - Granted in exchange for fear and reverence - True torah in their mouth - No iniquity on their lips - Turning many from iniquity - Mediator role between God and people ## NT fulfillment Hebrews argues the Levitical priesthood could not bring perfection and is supplanted by Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek (Heb 7:11-19), so the corrupted covenant Malachi mourns is replaced by a flawless mediator. ## Connects to `[[../Covenants/Priestly-Covenant-Phinehas]]`, `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/New-Covenant]]` ## Embedding hint The tribal-priestly teaching covenant — distinct from Phinehas in emphasizing instructional fidelity rather than zealous atonement.

to.title: High Priest
to.body: # High-Priest ## What this office is The high priest is the chief mediator of the covenant, the only one who enters the Most Holy Place — once a year on Yom Kippur — bearing blood for himself and the people. He wears the names of the twelve tribes on his breastpiece, representing all Israel before God. His person is intensely holy and his death releases the manslayer from the city of refuge. ## Typological significance / Christ as fulfillment Christ is the great High Priest who passed through the heavens (Heb 4:14), entering not an earthly Most Holy Place but heaven itself with His own blood. His priesthood is eternal, sinless, and effective — securing what Aaron's office only foreshadowed. ## Connects to `[[./Priest]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Symbols/Scapegoat]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint Office of supreme covenant mediator entering God's presence on behalf of the people.

prior_rationale: Solid echo via 'of Noah' (Νῶε); seed and seed share coherent theological vocabulary.

from.title: Flood Baptism
from.body: # Flood / Ark → Baptism Into Christ ## OT Story — Genesis 6-9 A righteous remnant is preserved in a wooden vessel as God's judgment washes the world clean. Noah's family passes through the waters of death and emerges into a new creation, greeted by a covenant rainbow. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Peter 3:20-21: "Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you" — through-water salvation in Christ's resurrection. - Matthew 24:37-39: The days of Noah typify unwatchful generations before the Son of Man's coming. - 2 Peter 2:5: Noah, "a herald of righteousness," prefigures the gospel preached before judgment. ## Shared structure - Judgment-bearing waters pass over a covenant household. - Wooden vessel as means of rescue (cf. cross, ark of Moses, ark of covenant). - New-creation emergence: dove, olive branch, altar. - Eight souls (1 Pet 3:20) — number of new beginning. ## Theological motif Death-And-Resurrection through water; salvation through judgment, not around it. ## Counterarguments 1. **Baptism is symbolic; the flood was judgment, not salvation.** *Peter's point is precisely that the waters that judged the world saved the family inside the ark — the same dynamic in baptism united to Christ.* 2. **Typology requires authorial intent.** *1 Peter is itself canonical authorial intent, sealing the linkage.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about passing through water unto salvation, judgment on the wicked alongside rescue of a remnant, or new-creation emergence after deluge.

to.title: Noahs Ark Salvation Through Judgment
to.body: # Noah's Ark / Salvation-Through-Judgment → Refuge in Christ ## OT Story — Genesis 6:13-22; 7:23 God commands Noah to build an ark of gopher wood pitched within and without. All who enter are saved through the very judgment that destroys the world. The door is shut by God himself (Gen 7:16). ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 11:7: Noah, warned by God, prepared an ark for the salvation of his household. - 1 Peter 3:20-21: Eight were brought safely through water — corresponding to baptism into Christ. - John 10:9: Christ is the door through which one is saved. ## Shared structure - A wooden structure of refuge sealed by God against wrath. - Single door — one means of entry (cf. John 10:9, John 14:6). - Pitch (Heb. kopher, same root as atonement, kippur) covering inside and out. - Salvation through judgment, not around it. ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice and Death-And-Resurrection — Christ is both ark and door, bearing wrath that those inside may live. ## Counterarguments 1. **The ark is just a means of survival.** *The lexical link kopher/kippur and the NT use make it a Christ-shaped vessel of atoning refuge.* 2. **Universal flood narratives appear in other ANE texts.** *Shared memory does not preclude theological design; Scripture interprets the event covenantally.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a wooden refuge, a single door of salvation, or being shut in by God against judgment.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ὁμοιότης' (the same way) appears in both 'Aarons Breastplate Twelve Stones' and 'Levitical Priest' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Aarons Breastplate Twelve Stones
from.body: # Aaron's Breastplate of Twelve Stones → Christ Carries His People on His Heart ## OT Story — Exodus 28:15-30 The high priest's breastplate bears twelve gemstones, each engraved with a tribe's name, worn over his heart whenever he enters the holy place. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 4:14-16: A great high priest who sympathizes — boldness to draw near. - Hebrews 7:25: He always lives to intercede. - Revelation 21:19-20: New Jerusalem's foundations are twelve precious stones. ## Shared structure - Tribes carried on the priest's heart into God's presence. - Twelve named individuals as foundation gemstones. - Intercession by representation. - From breastplate to city wall — the people endure in the priest's memorial. ## Theological motif High-priestly intercession by name. ## Counterarguments 1. **Stones are decorative.** *Their named engraving and 'memorial before the LORD' (Ex 28:29) make them theological.* 2. **Revelation's stones differ.** *Differences signal new-creation transformation, not negation, of the breastplate.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about names worn over the heart, twelve gemstones, or representative intercession.

to.title: Levitical Priest
to.body: # Levitical-Priest ## What this office is The Levitical priests are descendants of Aaron who serve at the altar, while the broader Levites assist with tabernacle/temple service, teaching Torah, and music. They have no tribal inheritance — YHWH is their portion — and live from the offerings of Israel. ## Typological significance / Christ as fulfillment The Levitical priesthood was provisional and weak — priests died and could not perfect worshipers (Heb 7). Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek supersedes it, accomplishing perpetual access where the Levitical system could only repeat sacrifice. ## Connects to `[[./High-Priest]]`, `[[./Priest]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint Office of hereditary cultic service tied to tabernacle/temple and Torah teaching.

prior_rationale: Solid echo via 'sins' (ἁμάρτημα); seed and festival share coherent theological vocabulary.

from.title: Mercy Seat Atonement
from.body: # Mercy Seat / Hilasterion → Christ Our Propitiation ## OT Story — Exodus 25:17-22; Leviticus 16:14-15 Over the ark sits the kapporet, the mercy seat, flanked by two cherubim. Once a year the high priest sprinkles blood there for the sins of the people. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 3:25: God put forward Christ as a propitiation (hilasterion). - Hebrews 9:5: The cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat. - 1 John 2:2: He is the propitiation for our sins. ## Shared structure - Blood applied at the place of God's throne. - Two cherubim, glory between (cf. John 20:12 at tomb). - Once-a-year access becomes once-for-all in Christ. - Throne of judgment becomes throne of mercy. ## Theological motif Propitiation — wrath turned aside by blood at the throne. ## Counterarguments 1. **'Hilasterion' may mean 'expiation,' not 'propitiation.'** *Both senses are present; the LXX uses it of the kapporet, anchoring propitiation.* 2. **Two cherubim and tomb-angels is too thin.** *John 20:12 deliberately echoes the mercy seat in resurrection language.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about blood at the mercy seat, propitiation, or two angels at the place of presence.

to.title: Day Of Atonement Yom Kippur
to.body: # Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) ## What it is The most solemn day of the year — the tenth of the seventh month — when the high priest entered the Holy of Holies with sacrificial blood to cleanse the sanctuary, the priesthood, and the people from a year of sin. ## Key elements - High priest alone enters the Most Holy Place - Two goats — one slain, one sent away - Sprinkling of blood on the mercy seat - Affliction of soul / fasting - Scapegoat bears iniquity into the wilderness ## NT fulfillment Hebrews structures its whole argument around Yom Kippur: Christ as the great high priest enters the heavenly sanctuary once for all by his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption (Heb 9). The two goats together prefigure his single sacrificial and sin-bearing work. ## Connects to `[[../Symbols/Scapegoat]]`, `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Festivals/Trumpets-Rosh-Hashanah]]`, `[[../Festivals/Tabernacles-Sukkot]]` ## Embedding hint The annual sanctuary-cleansing festival with paired goats — uniquely the only day the Holy of Holies is entered, semantically the substitution-and-removal of sin.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ἁλληλουϊά' (Hallelujah) links 'Feast Throughout Scripture' and 'Revelation 19 Marriage Supper Of Lamb' through covenant, creation, marriage, sacrifice.

from.title: Feast Throughout Scripture
from.body: # Feast Throughout Scripture ## NT Text — Matt 22; Luke 14; Rev 19 From Passover to Sinai's covenant meal, to Isaiah 25's feast on the mountain, to the Last Supper, to the marriage supper — feasts are eschatological signs. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 12** — Passover meal. - **Exod 24:9-11** — Covenant meal on Sinai. - **Isa 25:6** — Feast of fat things on this mountain. ## Shared structure - Shared table - Covenant ratified - Joy - Inclusion or exclusion - Eschatological foretaste ## Theological motif Feast is canon's primary picture of consummated communion. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why eating?** *Communion at the deepest creaturely level.* 2. **Inclusion how?** *By covenant — Lamb-blood applied.* ## Embedding hint A canon-wide feast pattern from Sinai's covenant meal to the Lamb's marriage feast.

to.title: Revelation 19 Marriage Supper Of Lamb
to.body: # Revelation 19: Marriage Supper of the Lamb ## NT Text — Rev 19:6-9 The wedding of the Lamb has come; his bride has made herself ready. The covenant-marriage of YHWH and Israel reaches its fulfillment. ## OT Antecedents - **Hos 2:14-23** — Betrothal of YHWH to Israel. - **Isa 62:5** — As bridegroom rejoices over bride. - **Ezek 16** — Covenant-marriage with Israel. ## Shared structure - Wedding feast - Bride prepared - Fine linen of saints - Blessed are invited - Lamb-bridegroom ## Theological motif Covenant-marriage typology culminated; church as bride received. ## Counterarguments 1. **Allegorical?** *Symbolic-but-real: union of Christ and church.* 2. **Earthly feast?** *Eternal communion-meal of which Eucharist is foretaste.* ## Embedding hint A wedding-supper of the Lamb fulfilling Hosea-Isaiah-Ezekiel bridal covenant.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ὡραῖος' (beautiful) appears in both 'Nahum Good News On Mountains' and 'Healing At Beautiful Gate' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Nahum Good News On Mountains
from.body: # Beautiful Feet of the Good-News-Bringer → Gospel Heralds ## OT Story — Nahum 1:15; Isaiah 52:7 'How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace, who says to Zion, Your God reigns!' ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 10:15: How beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news. - Ephesians 6:15: Feet shod with the readiness given by the gospel of peace. - Acts 10:36: The good news of peace through Jesus Christ. ## Shared structure - Herald with good news of peace. - Mountain-top proclamation. - 'Your God reigns' as kingdom-claim. - Feet of evangelists / apostolic mission. ## Theological motif Heralds of the kingdom — feet on mountains. ## Counterarguments 1. **Nahum is about Nineveh's fall.** *Yes — the good news for Judah is its corollary; Paul universalizes.* 2. **Imagery is poetic.** *Paul makes it grammar of the gospel mission.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about beautiful feet on the mountains, gospel-heralding, or 'your God reigns.'

to.title: Healing At Beautiful Gate
to.body: # Healing at the Beautiful Gate ## What happens Peter and John, going up to the temple, command a lame man at the Beautiful Gate to walk in the name of Jesus; he leaps and enters the temple praising God. ## Typological significance Isaiah 35's 'the lame shall leap' fulfilled at the very threshold of the temple — the kingdom invading the old sanctuary. Jesus' authority continues through His apostles in His name. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint Lame leaping at the temple gate — Isaiah's kingdom entering the sanctuary.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'Ἰεριχώ' (Jericho) appears in both 'Healing Of Blind Bartimaeus' and 'Good Samaritan' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Healing Of Blind Bartimaeus
from.body: # Healing of Blind Bartimaeus ## What happens Outside Jericho, the blind beggar Bartimaeus cries out 'Son of David, have mercy.' Jesus calls him; he throws off his cloak and is given sight, then follows Jesus on the way. ## Typological significance Confession of Davidic messiahship paired with restored sight — the messianic age of Isaiah 35 has arrived. The cloak cast off and the following 'on the way' to Jerusalem mark true discipleship. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Davidic confession answered by sight — discipleship 'on the way' to the cross.

to.title: Good Samaritan
to.body: # The Good Samaritan ## What happens A man is beaten by robbers on the Jerusalem-Jericho road; priest and Levite pass by, but a Samaritan binds his wounds, pays for his care, and promises to return. ## Typological significance Neighbor defined by mercy enacted, not by ethnic boundary. The despised outsider becomes the image of true covenant love — and, allegorically, of Christ Himself binding the wounds of fallen humanity. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Despised outsider as the true neighbor — mercy that crosses every line.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἱλασμός' ([the] propitiation); both units touch on messiah, sacrifice, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Day Of Atonement
from.body: # Day of Atonement → Christ's Atoning Work The most explicit and thoroughly-developed OT-to-NT typology in scripture. The book of Hebrews (chapters 7-10) is essentially an extended commentary on this very pattern. If any typology has *canonical-strength* warrant, this is it. ## OT Story — Leviticus 16 Once a year, on the **10th day of the 7th month** (Tishri), Israel observes Yom Kippur: 1. The high priest washes and dons plain linen (not his usual ornate garments) 2. He selects **two unblemished male goats** from the people 3. Lots are cast: one for *YHWH*, one for *Azazel* 4. He sacrifices a bull for his own sins, then enters the **Holy of Holies** 5. He sprinkles the bull's blood on the *kapporet* (mercy seat) above the ark 6. He slaughters the first goat and sprinkles its blood the same way — for the people's sin 7. He places his hands on the head of the second goat (the **scapegoat**), confessing all the sins of Israel onto it 8. The scapegoat is led into the wilderness "to a remote place" — sins symbolically removed from the camp 9. The remains of the sacrifices are burned **outside the camp** ## NT Fulfillment — Hebrews's extended interpretation - **Hebrews 9:11-12** — Christ "entered once for all into the Holy Places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" - **Hebrews 9:24-28** — Christ "appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself" - **Hebrews 10:1-4

to.title: 1 John 2 Atoning Sacrifice
to.body: # 1 John 2: Atoning Sacrifice ## NT Text — 1 John 2:1-2 If anyone sins we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins — and for the whole world. ## OT Antecedents - **Lev 16** — Day of Atonement propitiation. - **Isa 53:10** — Soul an offering for sin. - **Ps 32:1-2** — Blessed is he whose sin is covered. ## Shared structure - Advocate - Propitiation - Universal scope - Sin-cleansing ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice as ongoing intercession-base; mercy-seat applied. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universal atonement?** *Sufficient for all; effectual for believers.* 2. **Advocacy vs. once-for-all?** *Sacrifice once; intercession ongoing.* ## Embedding hint A propitiation-and-advocate scheme rooting Christian assurance in Christ's atoning blood.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'lamb' (freq 4) is central to one side; theme overlap: lamb_sacrifice, suffering_servant.

from.title: Two Goats Yom Kippur
from.body: # Two Goats of Atonement → Christ's Death and Bearing-Away ## OT Story — Leviticus 16:7-22 On Yom Kippur, two goats: one slaughtered as sin offering, its blood taken behind the veil; the other (the scapegoat) bears confessed sins into the wilderness, never to return. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 9:11-14: Christ entered the holy places by his own blood. - Hebrews 10:11-14: One offering for sins forever. - John 1:29: The Lamb of God who takes away (bears away) the sin of the world. ## Shared structure - Two complementary aspects of one atonement. - Blood applied / sin removed. - Wilderness as place of expulsion. - Annual repetition replaced by once-for-all. ## Theological motif Substitution and removal in one event at the cross. ## Counterarguments 1. **Two goats = two distinct theologies.** *They are commanded as a unity, fulfilled in one person.* 2. **Azazel-goat may be a demon-tribute.** *Even if so, the NT redirects: sin sent back where it came from.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about two goats, blood within / sins removed, or Christ bearing away sin.

to.title: Suffering Servant Isaiah 53
to.body: # Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 ## The oracle The Servant is despised, pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; like a lamb led to slaughter, he bears the sin of many and, after suffering, sees light and is exalted. ## Date / context Fourth Servant Song in Isaiah's exilic comfort section, late 8th c. BC, addressed to a future people who confess their misjudgment of him. ## Fulfillment Philip preaches Jesus from this passage to the Ethiopian (Acts 8); Peter applies it to Christ's atoning death (1 Peter 2:21-25); Matthew sees the healings as its initial outworking (Matt 8:17). ## Connects to `[[../Titles/Suffering-Servant]]`, `[[../Titles/Lamb-Of-God]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Symbols/Lamb]]` ## Embedding hint The clearest pre-Christian articulation of vicarious atonement — penal substitution in poetic form.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'μεσίτης' (of a mediator) appears in both 'Hebrews 12 Mount Zion Not Sinai' and 'Job Mediator Daysman' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Hebrews 12 Mount Zion Not Sinai
from.body: # Hebrews 12: Mount Zion, Not Sinai ## NT Text — Heb 12:18-24 You have not come to Sinai with its terror but to Mount Zion, to the heavenly Jerusalem, to angels, to the assembly of the firstborn, to Jesus the mediator. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 19** — Sinai theophany of terror. - **Ps 48** — Zion's beauty. - **Isa 60** — Zion glorified. ## Shared structure - Sinai/terror contrast - Zion/grace destination - Heavenly Jerusalem - Mediator and sprinkled blood ## Theological motif Sinai-Zion typology fulfilled; the church already participates in heavenly worship. ## Counterarguments 1. **Replaces Sinai?** *Fulfills, doesn't repudiate.* 2. **Already-not-yet?** *Heavenly Jerusalem is real present, fuller future.* ## Embedding hint A Sinai-to-Zion contrast locating the church already in heavenly worship.

to.title: Job Mediator Daysman
to.body: # Job's Cry for a Daysman → Christ Our Mediator ## OT Story — Job 9:32-35; 16:19-21 Job: 'There is no umpire between us… Even now my witness is in heaven; he who testifies for me is on high. Oh, that one might plead for a man with God!' He longs for a daysman to lay his hand on both. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Timothy 2:5: One mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. - Hebrews 7:25: He always lives to intercede. - Hebrews 9:24: He has entered heaven itself to appear in God's presence for us. ## Shared structure - Awareness of the gap between God and man. - Cry for an arbiter. - Witness in heaven foreshadowed. - Faith reaching beyond available means. ## Theological motif Mediation — the gospel answer to Job's cry. ## Counterarguments 1. **Job speaks in despair.** *His faith breaks through despair; canon affirms.* 2. **Daysman is poetic, not prophetic.** *Hebrews 9:24 makes the canonical answer explicit.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a mediator between God and man, witness in heaven, or daysman.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἦχος (I)' (the report) appears in both 'Glory On Sinai' and 'Pentecost Fire And Wind' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Glory On Sinai
from.body: # Glory on Sinai ## The appearance The mountain is wrapped in thick cloud, thunder, lightning, and trumpet blast; YHWH descends in fire, the mountain quakes, and Moses ascends into the cloud where the glory dwells; later YHWH passes by, proclaiming his name. ## Mode / context Covenant-making at Sinai; the constitutive theophany of Israel. ## Significance The encounter that founds the covenant and the law; pattern of all subsequent glory-theophanies. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Places/Sinai]]` ## Embedding hint The fullest OT theophany — sound, fire, smoke, and name — covenantal in form.

to.title: Pentecost Fire And Wind
to.body: # Pentecost — Fire and Wind ## The appearance A sound like a mighty rushing wind fills the house; tongues as of fire appear, distributed and resting on each of them; all are filled with the Holy Spirit and begin to speak in other tongues. ## Mode / context Fiftieth day after Passover, c. AD 30, in Jerusalem. ## Significance The Sinai theophany (fire, wind, voices) reapplied to the church; the new-covenant Spirit indwells all flesh. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint Sinai inverted: instead of one mediator ascending to fire, fire descends on many.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἀμνός' (Lamb) appears in both 'Plague Of Firstborn Spared By Blood' and 'Suffering Servant Isaiah 53' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Plague Of Firstborn Spared By Blood
from.body: # Plague of the Firstborn / Blood on the Door → Christ's Blood Spares ## OT Story — Exodus 12:21-30 Israel slaughters the Passover lamb, strikes its blood on the doorposts, and stays inside. The destroyer passes over houses marked by blood. Egypt's firstborn die. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Corinthians 5:7: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed. - 1 Peter 1:18-19: Redeemed by the precious blood of a lamb without blemish. - John 1:29: Behold the Lamb of God. ## Shared structure - Lamb slain in place of the firstborn. - Blood marking the household. - Inside-the-house safety while judgment passes by. - Bones not broken (cf. John 19:36). ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice — life for life via the lamb. ## Counterarguments 1. **Passover is national, not personal.** *The household structure makes it both; the NT reads it personally and corporately.* 2. **Bones-not-broken is coincidental.** *John 19:36 explicitly cites Exodus 12 as fulfilled.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about blood on doorposts, the destroyer passing over, or unbroken bones of the lamb.

to.title: Suffering Servant Isaiah 53
to.body: # Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 ## The oracle The Servant is despised, pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; like a lamb led to slaughter, he bears the sin of many and, after suffering, sees light and is exalted. ## Date / context Fourth Servant Song in Isaiah's exilic comfort section, late 8th c. BC, addressed to a future people who confess their misjudgment of him. ## Fulfillment Philip preaches Jesus from this passage to the Ethiopian (Acts 8); Peter applies it to Christ's atoning death (1 Peter 2:21-25); Matthew sees the healings as its initial outworking (Matt 8:17). ## Connects to `[[../Titles/Suffering-Servant]]`, `[[../Titles/Lamb-Of-God]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Symbols/Lamb]]` ## Embedding hint The clearest pre-Christian articulation of vicarious atonement — penal substitution in poetic form.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'אֵיבָה' (enmity) appears in both 'Words From Cross Woman Behold Son' and 'Protoevangelium Genesis 3 15' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Words From Cross Woman Behold Son
from.body: # Word from Cross: Woman, Behold Your Son ## NT Text — John 19:26-27 Jesus entrusts his mother to the beloved disciple. The new-covenant family is constituted at the cross. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 3:15** — Woman whose seed crushes serpent. - **Ps 22:9-10** — Mother who bore me. - **Ruth 4:14-17** — Naomi receives son via kinsman-redemption. ## Shared structure - Mother and son - New familial bond - Care entrusted - Womanhood foregrounded ## Theological motif Kinsman-Redeemer creates new family at the cross. ## Counterarguments 1. **Did Jesus have siblings?** *Yes; spiritual succession is signaled.* 2. **Mariology?** *Mary as model believer here.* ## Embedding hint A cross-side adoption constituting new-covenant kinship.

to.title: Protoevangelium Genesis 3 15
to.body: # Protoevangelium (Genesis 3:15) ## The oracle God curses the serpent and declares enmity between the serpent's seed and the woman's seed; the seed will crush the serpent's head while having his heel bruised. The "first gospel" announces, within the judgment of the fall, the coming of a redeemer. ## Date / context Spoken by YHWH in Eden immediately after the fall, addressed to the serpent but heard by Adam and Eve. It frames all subsequent redemptive history. ## Fulfillment The seed is narrowed through Seth, Shem, Abraham, Judah, and David, and culminates in Christ (Galatians 4:4). The cross is the heel-bruise; the resurrection and final crushing of Satan (Romans 16:20; Revelation 20) is the head-crush. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Adam]]`, `[[../Places/Eden]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Motifs/Gospel-In-Narrative]]` ## Embedding hint The very first messianic promise, embedded in a curse, establishing the seed-of-the-woman motif that organizes the entire canon.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'λουτρόν' (washing) appears in both 'Ezekiel 36 New Heart' and 'New Heart Ezekiel 36' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Ezekiel 36 New Heart
from.body: # Ezekiel 36 / New Heart, New Spirit → Born of Water and Spirit ## OT Story — Ezekiel 36:25-27 'I will sprinkle clean water on you… A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you. I will remove the heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh.' ## NT Fulfillment - John 3:5-8: Born of water and Spirit. - Titus 3:5: Washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit. - 2 Corinthians 3:3: Tablets of human hearts. ## Shared structure - Clean water of purification. - Stone heart replaced by heart of flesh. - Spirit indwelling. - Walk in God's statutes empowered. ## Theological motif Regeneration in the new covenant. ## Counterarguments 1. **Water in Ezekiel is ceremonial.** *John 3 makes the canonical move to spiritual cleansing.* 2. **Heart-language is metaphor.** *Yes, and the metaphor names the deepest reality.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about clean water, heart of flesh replacing stone, or Spirit put within.

to.title: New Heart Ezekiel 36
to.body: # New Heart (Ezekiel 36) ## The oracle "I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean… A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you… and I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes." ## Date / context Ezekiel's restoration oracle to the exiles. ## Fulfillment Jesus assumes it as common knowledge with Nicodemus ("born of water and Spirit," John 3:5); Paul echoes the washing-of-regeneration language (Titus 3:5). ## Connects to (no narrower stems) ## Embedding hint The triad of cleansing, new heart, and indwelling Spirit — the anatomy of regeneration.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'λέων' (of the lion) appears in both 'Jacobs Blessing Judah Lion' and 'Daniel 7 Four Beasts' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Jacobs Blessing Judah Lion
from.body: # Judah the Lion / Shiloh → Lion of Judah, Messianic King ## OT Story — Genesis 49:8-12 Jacob declares of Judah: a lion's cub, scepter not departing until Shiloh comes; he binds his foal to the vine, washes his garments in wine. Tribes will obey him. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 5:5: The Lion of the tribe of Judah has conquered. - Hebrews 7:14: Our Lord descended from Judah. - Matthew 21:5: The king comes on a donkey — Genesis 49:11 imagery. - Revelation 19:13: Garments dipped in blood. ## Shared structure - Lion as royal Messiah-image. - Scepter / unwavering rule until 'Shiloh' (he whose right it is). - Donkey tethered to the vine — gospel triumphal entry. - Garments stained with wine/blood — cross and judgment. ## Theological motif Messianic kingship rooted in Jacob's deathbed. ## Counterarguments 1. **Shiloh is a place, not a person.** *Ancient versions and rabbinic readings agree on a Messianic referent; the NT echoes confirm.* 2. **Wine in garments is just abundance.** *Revelation 19 explicitly links to blood-stained robes; canonical reading consolidates both.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about the Lion of Judah, scepter, donkey of the king, or wine/blood-stained robes.

to.title: Daniel 7 Four Beasts
to.body: # Daniel 7 — Four Beasts ## The oracle Daniel sees four great beasts arising from the chaotic sea — lion, bear, leopard, and a dreadful fourth — representing four kingdoms; the little horn of the fourth speaks great things and wages war against the saints. ## Date / context Vision in the first year of Belshazzar, c. 553 BC. ## Fulfillment Traditionally identified with Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, Rome; Revelation 13 composites the imagery into the eschatological beast. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint Imperial succession imaged as bestial chaos — the political order under judgment.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'jesse' (freq 5) is central to one side; theme overlap: lamb_sacrifice, temple.

from.title: Isaiah 11 Branch Of Jesse
from.body: # Branch from Jesse's Stump → Peaceable Kingdom of the Root ## OT Story — Isaiah 11:1-10 From the stump of Jesse a shoot comes; the Spirit rests on him. He judges with righteousness. The wolf dwells with the lamb. The earth is full of the knowledge of the LORD. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 15:12: 'The root of Jesse will come; in him the Gentiles will hope.' - Revelation 5:5: The Root of David has conquered. - Revelation 22:16: I am the root and descendant of David. ## Shared structure - Dynasty cut down, yet new shoot. - Spirit-anointed righteous judge. - Cosmic peace under his reign. - Gentile ingathering to the root. ## Theological motif Branch and root — Messianic restoration. ## Counterarguments 1. **Peaceable kingdom is allegorical.** *Whether eschatological or new-creation literal, the canon takes it as Messianic horizon.* 2. **Branch is multiple figures.** *Yes — branch language coalesces in the singular fulfillment (Jer 23; Zech 6).* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a branch / shoot from David, peaceable kingdom, or Gentiles hoping in the root.

to.title: Rahab Scarlet Cord
to.body: # Rahab's Scarlet Cord → Blood-Marked Household Saved ## OT Story — Joshua 2; 6:22-25 Rahab the Canaanite prostitute hides the spies and asks for mercy. They give her a scarlet cord to hang from her window; only those in her house with the cord will be spared when Jericho falls. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 11:31: By faith Rahab did not perish. - James 2:25: She was justified by works that demonstrated her faith. - Matthew 1:5: Rahab is in Christ's genealogy. ## Shared structure - Scarlet sign on the house — echoes Passover blood. - Gentile sinner saved by faith. - Household salvation. - Incorporated into the covenant line. ## Theological motif Faith-driven Gentile inclusion under the scarlet sign. ## Counterarguments 1. **Scarlet is just identification.** *Its Passover echo and patristic reading (Clement of Rome) make it typological.* 2. **Rahab's faith is minimal.** *Hebrews and James both treat it as exemplary; canon endorses.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a scarlet sign, household saved, or Gentile-prostitute justified by faith.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ἐγκαινίζω' (has been inaugurated) links 'Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant' and 'Veil Of Tabernacle' through blood, exodus, sacrifice.

from.title: Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant
from.body: # Hebrews 9: Mediator of the New Covenant ## NT Text — Heb 9:15-22 Christ is mediator of a new covenant. Where a covenant exists, the death of the covenant-maker must be established. Sinai's blood-sprinkling foreshadowed Calvary's. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 24:6-8** — Moses sprinkles blood on the people. - **Jer 31:33** — New covenant. - **Gen 15:9-21** — Covenant-cutting with Abraham. ## Shared structure - Covenant established by death - Blood-sprinkling - Sin-bearing - Eternal inheritance secured ## Theological motif Sinai-covenant inauguration prefigures the new; substitutionary-sacrifice covenant-blood is Christ's own. ## Counterarguments 1. **Diatheke = will or covenant?** *Pun deliberate — both senses are operative.* 2. **Why blood-mediation?** *Life-for-life logic established in Lev 17.* ## Embedding hint A new-covenant mediation argument grounding inauguration in covenant-cutting blood.

to.title: Veil Of Tabernacle
to.body: # Veil of the Tabernacle Torn → New and Living Way ## OT Story — Exodus 26:31-33 A heavy veil with cherubim separates the holy place from the most holy. Only the high priest enters, once a year, with blood. ## NT Fulfillment - Matthew 27:51: At Christ's death the veil is torn from top to bottom. - Hebrews 10:19-22: We enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way opened through the veil — his flesh. - Ephesians 2:18: Through him we both have access in one Spirit. ## Shared structure - Veil bars sinful access. - Cherubim embroidered — same guardians as at Eden's gate. - Tearing top-to-bottom = divine initiative. - Christ's flesh as the veil rent open. ## Theological motif Access to God opened through the torn flesh of Christ. ## Counterarguments 1. **Symbolic equation flesh=veil is fanciful.** *Hebrews 10:20 makes the equation explicitly canonical.* 2. **Top-to-bottom is incidental detail.** *Matthew's emphasis is theological; God tears, not man.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a torn veil, new access to the holy, or Christ's flesh as the way in.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'κρύσταλλος' (crystal) appears in both 'Ezekiel 1 Throne Chariot' and 'Living Waters Zechariah' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Ezekiel 1 Throne Chariot
from.body: # Throne Chariot / Merkavah → Revelation's Throne Vision ## OT Story — Ezekiel 1 Ezekiel sees a stormy theophany — four living creatures, wheels within wheels, a throne of sapphire, a figure of fire and rainbow. The glory of the LORD. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 4:6-8: Four living creatures around the throne, full of eyes. - Revelation 4:3: Around the throne a rainbow. - Isaiah 6:1-3: Throne with seraphim crying 'Holy.' ## Shared structure - Throne mobile — God present in exile. - Four creatures, eyes, wheels. - Fire and rainbow encircling. - Voice from the throne. ## Theological motif Throne theology of presence in exile. ## Counterarguments 1. **Ezekiel's vision is unique.** *Revelation deliberately rebuilds it for Christ-on-throne.* 2. **Merkavah mysticism is speculative.** *Revelation's canonical rereading is not speculative.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a throne with four living creatures, wheels of glory, or rainbow around the throne.

to.title: Living Waters Zechariah
to.body: # Living Waters from Jerusalem ## The oracle On that day living waters shall flow out from Jerusalem, half to the eastern sea and half to the western sea, summer and winter alike. ## Date / context Zechariah 14, post-exilic; Ezekiel 47 had earlier described temple waters healing the Dead Sea. ## Fulfillment John applies "rivers of living water" to the Spirit (John 7:38-39); Revelation 22 sees the river of the water of life flowing from the throne in the New Jerusalem. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint The eschatological hydrology — Eden's rivers restored from the new temple-city.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'lamb' (freq 4) is central to one side; theme overlap: lamb_sacrifice, covenant.

from.title: Plague Of Firstborn Spared By Blood
from.body: # Plague of the Firstborn / Blood on the Door → Christ's Blood Spares ## OT Story — Exodus 12:21-30 Israel slaughters the Passover lamb, strikes its blood on the doorposts, and stays inside. The destroyer passes over houses marked by blood. Egypt's firstborn die. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Corinthians 5:7: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed. - 1 Peter 1:18-19: Redeemed by the precious blood of a lamb without blemish. - John 1:29: Behold the Lamb of God. ## Shared structure - Lamb slain in place of the firstborn. - Blood marking the household. - Inside-the-house safety while judgment passes by. - Bones not broken (cf. John 19:36). ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice — life for life via the lamb. ## Counterarguments 1. **Passover is national, not personal.** *The household structure makes it both; the NT reads it personally and corporately.* 2. **Bones-not-broken is coincidental.** *John 19:36 explicitly cites Exodus 12 as fulfilled.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about blood on doorposts, the destroyer passing over, or unbroken bones of the lamb.

to.title: Blood Covenant
to.body: # Blood Covenant ## What it is The general scriptural form of covenant ratification through the shedding and application of blood, signaling that the parties bind themselves under the penalty of death for unfaithfulness. ## Key elements - Cutting and passing between pieces (Gen 15) - Sprinkling on people and altar (Ex 24) - Self-maledictory oath - Atonement through life-for-life substitution - Inviolable bond once ratified ## NT fulfillment Hebrews argues that without shedding of blood there is no forgiveness, and Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary by his own blood to inaugurate the eternal covenant (Heb 9). Jesus identifies his cup as "the blood of the covenant poured out for many" (Matt 26:28). ## Connects to `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Symbols/Lamb]]`, `[[../Covenants/Abrahamic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/New-Covenant]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint The generic blood-ratified covenant form — semantically distinct as the underlying ritual grammar that all specific biblical covenants instantiate.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἐξαγοράζω' (redeemed); both units touch on creation, judgment, messiah, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Galatians 3 Curse Of Law
from.body: # Galatians 3: Curse of the Law ## NT Text — Gal 3:10-14 Christ became a curse for us, hanging on a tree — fulfilling Deut 21:23 and Deut 27:26. The curse of the law is borne so that Abrahamic blessing flows to the nations. ## OT Antecedents - **Deut 21:23** — Cursed is everyone hanged on a tree. - **Deut 27:26** — Cursed is anyone who does not uphold this law. - **Gen 12:3** — Abrahamic blessing to nations. ## Shared structure - Hanging on tree - Curse-bearing - Substitutionary exchange - Blessing flowing to Gentiles ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice on the Wood; Abrahamic promise unleashed. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is the curse-bearing literal?** *Theologically yes; the cross is the curse-altar.* 2. **Why Abraham here?** *Abrahamic blessing precedes Sinai-curse.* ## Embedding hint A curse-bearing on the tree fulfilling Deut 21:23 to release Abrahamic blessing to the nations.

to.title: Tree Throughout Scripture
to.body: # Tree Throughout Scripture ## NT Text — Acts 5:30; Gal 3:13; Rev 22 Eden's two trees, Abraham's oaks, Israel's vine and olive, the tree of curse on which Christ hung, the tree of life in Revelation. Trees thread sin and salvation. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 2:9** — Tree of life and tree of knowledge. - **Gen 21:33** — Abraham's tamarisk. - **Deut 21:23** — Cursed is anyone hung on a tree. - **Ezek 47:12** — Trees on river-banks for healing. ## Shared structure - Tree as place of decision - Tree as cross - Tree as life - Tree of healing ## Theological motif Tree of curse (cross) reverses the tree of disobedience and restores access to the tree of life. ## Counterarguments 1. **Wood and tree distinct?** *Overlapping symbol-set; both Wood and Tree of Life implicated.* 2. **Trees in eschaton?** *Yes — Rev 22 makes plant-life part of the consummation.* ## Embedding hint A canon-wide tree pattern from Eden's two trees to the cross-tree to the tree of life.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'τράγος' (of goats); both units touch on blood, bread, messiah, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Day Of Atonement High Priest
from.body: # High Priest on the Day of Atonement → Christ's Greater Entry ## OT Story — Leviticus 16 Once a year the high priest, after washing and offering for his own sin, enters the most holy place with blood, sprinkles the mercy seat, and emerges to bless the people. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 9:6-12: Christ entered once for all, with his own blood, into the greater tent not made with hands. - Hebrews 4:14-16: We have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens. - Hebrews 9:28: He will appear a second time to those who eagerly wait. ## Shared structure - Annual / once-for-all. - Animal blood / own blood. - Earthly sanctuary / heavenly. - Emergence to bless the waiting people. ## Theological motif Christ as high priest and offering on the day of cosmic atonement. ## Counterarguments 1. **Hebrews allegorizes the rite.** *Hebrews reads it typologically with explicit warrant, naming both rite and fulfillment.* 2. **Two-stage entry-and-return is over-read.** *Hebrews 9:28 names exactly this pattern.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a high priest entering with blood, once-for-all sacrifice, or emergence to bless.

to.title: Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant
to.body: # Hebrews 9: Mediator of the New Covenant ## NT Text — Heb 9:15-22 Christ is mediator of a new covenant. Where a covenant exists, the death of the covenant-maker must be established. Sinai's blood-sprinkling foreshadowed Calvary's. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 24:6-8** — Moses sprinkles blood on the people. - **Jer 31:33** — New covenant. - **Gen 15:9-21** — Covenant-cutting with Abraham. ## Shared structure - Covenant established by death - Blood-sprinkling - Sin-bearing - Eternal inheritance secured ## Theological motif Sinai-covenant inauguration prefigures the new; substitutionary-sacrifice covenant-blood is Christ's own. ## Counterarguments 1. **Diatheke = will or covenant?** *Pun deliberate — both senses are operative.* 2. **Why blood-mediation?** *Life-for-life logic established in Lev 17.* ## Embedding hint A new-covenant mediation argument grounding inauguration in covenant-cutting blood.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἱλασμός' ([the] propitiation) appears in both '1 John 2 Atoning Sacrifice' and 'hilasmos' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: 1 John 2 Atoning Sacrifice
from.body: # 1 John 2: Atoning Sacrifice ## NT Text — 1 John 2:1-2 If anyone sins we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins — and for the whole world. ## OT Antecedents - **Lev 16** — Day of Atonement propitiation. - **Isa 53:10** — Soul an offering for sin. - **Ps 32:1-2** — Blessed is he whose sin is covered. ## Shared structure - Advocate - Propitiation - Universal scope - Sin-cleansing ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice as ongoing intercession-base; mercy-seat applied. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universal atonement?** *Sufficient for all; effectual for believers.* 2. **Advocacy vs. once-for-all?** *Sacrifice once; intercession ongoing.* ## Embedding hint A propitiation-and-advocate scheme rooting Christian assurance in Christ's atoning blood.

to.title: hilasmos
to.body: # hilasmos (ἱλασμός) — propitiation ## Semantic field The means of turning aside wrath or wiping away sin (debate: propitiation vs expiation). Related noun *hilasterion* names the mercy seat / atonement cover. ## Theological weight "He is the hilasmos for our sins" (1 John 2:2). Christ is both the priest who offers and the offering that turns aside divine wrath while expiating sin's defilement. ## Septuagint mapping Renders Hebrew kapparah / kipper. ## Embedding hint Atoning sacrifice that addresses both God's wrath and humanity's defilement.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'of bones' (ὀστέον); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and prophecy worth surfacing.

from.title: Bones Of Joseph Carried Out
from.body: # Bones of Joseph Carried Out → Hope of Resurrection ## OT Story — Genesis 50:24-26 Dying in Egypt, Joseph commands: 'God will surely visit you; carry up my bones from here.' Moses takes the bones at the exodus; Joshua buries them in the land. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 11:22: By faith Joseph spoke of the exodus and gave instructions concerning his bones. - Ezekiel 37: Dry bones live by the Spirit. - 1 Corinthians 15:20: Christ the firstfruits of those who sleep. ## Shared structure - Faith in promised land beyond death. - Bones preserved for resurrection-day return. - Joseph's exit anticipates Israel's exodus and a greater bodily future. - The covenant binds the dead to the living promise. ## Theological motif Hope of resurrection embedded in patriarchal faith. ## Counterarguments 1. **Bone-burial is just custom.** *Hebrews 11:22 frames it explicitly as faith in God's future visitation.* 2. **This is not resurrection per se.** *The seed of bodily hope in the land matures into resurrection theology.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about bones preserved, faith in future visitation, or burial in the land of promise.

to.title: Bones Not Broken
to.body: # Bones Not Broken ## The oracle "He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken" (Psalm 34:20), echoing the Passover statute that no bone of the lamb be broken (Exodus 12:46). ## Date / context Davidic psalm and mosaic Passover regulation, joined typologically. ## Fulfillment John records that the soldiers did not break Jesus' legs because he was already dead, and cites the verse explicitly (John 19:33-36). ## Connects to `[[../Places/Calvary]]`, `[[../Titles/Lamb-Of-God]]`, `[[../Symbols/Passover-Lamb]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint A liturgical detail of the Passover lamb fulfilled in crucifixion mechanics.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'Jesse' (Ἰεσσαί); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and prophecy worth surfacing.

from.title: David Anointed Youngest
from.body: # David Anointed in Bethlehem → Christ Anointed ## OT Story — 1 Samuel 16:1-13 Samuel comes to Bethlehem. Jesse parades seven sons; God chooses none. David, the youngest, is summoned from the sheep. Samuel anoints him; the Spirit rushes upon him. ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 13:22: God raised David to be king, a man after his own heart. - Luke 4:18: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me; he has anointed me. - 1 Samuel 16:7: The LORD looks at the heart. ## Shared structure - Bethlehem birth/anointing. - Younger / overlooked chosen. - Spirit comes upon the anointed. - Shepherd-king pattern. ## Theological motif Anointed king from Bethlehem. ## Counterarguments 1. **David's anointing is local.** *Its messianic resonance is canonical and gospel-explicit.* 2. **Eight sons may be different count.** *Numbering varies but the youngest-chosen pattern stands.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about Bethlehem anointing, the youngest/overlooked, or Spirit-anointed king.

to.title: Branch Of Jesse
to.body: # Branch of Jesse ## The oracle A shoot shall come from the stump of Jesse and a branch from his roots, upon whom the Spirit of YHWH rests; he will judge righteously, and the nations will seek him. ## Date / context Isaiah's oracle to Judah amid Assyrian threat, late 8th c. BC; the Branch motif recurs in Jeremiah and Zechariah after the exile. ## Fulfillment Paul applies it to Christ, in whom the Gentiles hope (Romans 15:12); Jesus is the Root and Offspring of David (Revelation 22:16). ## Connects to `[[../Persons/David]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint Messianic shoot rising from a felled dynasty — restoration after judgment.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἐγκαινίζω' (has been inaugurated); both units touch on bread, messiah, resurrection, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant
from.body: # Hebrews 9: Mediator of the New Covenant ## NT Text — Heb 9:15-22 Christ is mediator of a new covenant. Where a covenant exists, the death of the covenant-maker must be established. Sinai's blood-sprinkling foreshadowed Calvary's. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 24:6-8** — Moses sprinkles blood on the people. - **Jer 31:33** — New covenant. - **Gen 15:9-21** — Covenant-cutting with Abraham. ## Shared structure - Covenant established by death - Blood-sprinkling - Sin-bearing - Eternal inheritance secured ## Theological motif Sinai-covenant inauguration prefigures the new; substitutionary-sacrifice covenant-blood is Christ's own. ## Counterarguments 1. **Diatheke = will or covenant?** *Pun deliberate — both senses are operative.* 2. **Why blood-mediation?** *Life-for-life logic established in Lev 17.* ## Embedding hint A new-covenant mediation argument grounding inauguration in covenant-cutting blood.

to.title: Veil Of Temple Torn
to.body: # Veil of the Temple Torn ## NT Text — Matt 27:51 At Christ's death the temple veil tears top to bottom. The barrier between holy and most-holy is rent. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 26:31-33** — Veil separating holy from most-holy. - **Lev 16:2** — Aaron enters only on Day of Atonement. - **Isa 25:7** — He will destroy the covering. ## Shared structure - Top-down tearing - Sanctuary access - End of separation - Cosmic accompanying signs ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice opens the holiest. Heb 10:20 calls the veil his flesh. ## Counterarguments 1. **Literally torn?** *Synoptics agree; rabbinic hints exist.* 2. **Access universal?** *Yes — through the Mediator.* ## Embedding hint A top-down veil-rending signifying inaugurated sanctuary access through torn flesh.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'συντρίβω' (bruised) appears in both 'Zechariah 12 They Will Look On Him' and 'Pierced One Zechariah' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Zechariah 12 They Will Look On Him
from.body: # They Will Look on Him Whom They Pierced → John 19; Revelation 1 ## OT Story — Zechariah 12:10 'I will pour out on the house of David a spirit of grace and pleas for mercy, so that when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced, they shall mourn.' ## NT Fulfillment - John 19:34-37: Soldier pierced his side; Scripture fulfilled. - Revelation 1:7: Every eye will see him, even those who pierced him. - Matthew 24:30: Tribes of the earth will mourn. ## Shared structure - First-person God speaks of being pierced. - Mourning paired with grace outpoured. - Personal recognition of the pierced one. - Eschatological seeing in Revelation. ## Theological motif The pierced one is divine; mourning leads to mercy. ## Counterarguments 1. **Pronouns shift confusingly.** *The shift is the very mystery: God says 'me… him' of the pierced one.* 2. **Two NT uses differ.** *John for first coming, Revelation for second — both inhabit the same text.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about looking on the pierced one, mourning leading to mercy, or 'every eye will see him.'

to.title: Pierced One Zechariah
to.body: # Pierced One (Zechariah 12:10) ## The oracle YHWH pours out a spirit of grace and supplication on Jerusalem so that they look on "me whom they have pierced" and mourn for him as for an only son. ## Date / context Post-exilic oracle of Zechariah, late 6th c. BC, looking to a future day of repentance. ## Fulfillment John applies it to the spear-thrust at the cross (John 19:34-37); Revelation 1:7 uses it of the second coming, when all tribes will mourn. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint YHWH speaks as the one pierced — the identity of the suffering one with God himself.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word '[the] propitiation' (freq 2) is central to both; theme overlap: lamb_sacrifice.

from.title: 1 John 2 Atoning Sacrifice
from.body: # 1 John 2: Atoning Sacrifice ## NT Text — 1 John 2:1-2 If anyone sins we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins — and for the whole world. ## OT Antecedents - **Lev 16** — Day of Atonement propitiation. - **Isa 53:10** — Soul an offering for sin. - **Ps 32:1-2** — Blessed is he whose sin is covered. ## Shared structure - Advocate - Propitiation - Universal scope - Sin-cleansing ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice as ongoing intercession-base; mercy-seat applied. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universal atonement?** *Sufficient for all; effectual for believers.* 2. **Advocacy vs. once-for-all?** *Sacrifice once; intercession ongoing.* ## Embedding hint A propitiation-and-advocate scheme rooting Christian assurance in Christ's atoning blood.

to.title: Mercy Seat Atonement
to.body: # Mercy Seat / Hilasterion → Christ Our Propitiation ## OT Story — Exodus 25:17-22; Leviticus 16:14-15 Over the ark sits the kapporet, the mercy seat, flanked by two cherubim. Once a year the high priest sprinkles blood there for the sins of the people. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 3:25: God put forward Christ as a propitiation (hilasterion). - Hebrews 9:5: The cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat. - 1 John 2:2: He is the propitiation for our sins. ## Shared structure - Blood applied at the place of God's throne. - Two cherubim, glory between (cf. John 20:12 at tomb). - Once-a-year access becomes once-for-all in Christ. - Throne of judgment becomes throne of mercy. ## Theological motif Propitiation — wrath turned aside by blood at the throne. ## Counterarguments 1. **'Hilasterion' may mean 'expiation,' not 'propitiation.'** *Both senses are present; the LXX uses it of the kapporet, anchoring propitiation.* 2. **Two cherubim and tomb-angels is too thin.** *John 20:12 deliberately echoes the mercy seat in resurrection language.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about blood at the mercy seat, propitiation, or two angels at the place of presence.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ἐκκεντέω' (they have pierced) links 'Daniel 7 Son Of Man' and 'Pierced Side Blood And Water' through water.

from.title: Daniel 7 Son Of Man
from.body: # Daniel 7 Son of Man → Jesus' Self-Identification ## OT Story — Daniel 7:9-14 Daniel sees a night vision. Four monstrous beasts arise from the chaotic sea; they represent earthly empires. Then the scene shifts to a heavenly throne room: *"As I looked, thrones were placed, and the **Ancient of Days** took his seat ... the court sat in judgment, and the books were opened ... and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came **one like a son of man**, and he came to the Ancient of Days and was presented before him. And to him was given **dominion and glory and a kingdom**, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him; his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and his kingdom one that shall not be destroyed"* (Dan 7:9-14). A human figure approaches the divine throne on the clouds — a movement reserved in the OT for God himself (cf. Ps 104:3; Isa 19:1) — and receives universal worship and eternal dominion. ## NT Fulfillment - **Mark 14:62 / Matthew 26:64** — Jesus, at his trial before the high priest, when asked if he is the Christ, replies: *"I am, and you will see the Son of Man **seated at the right hand of Power, and coming with the clouds of heaven**."* This is a deliberate, fused citation of **Psalm 110:1 + Daniel 7:13**. The high priest tears his clothes and charges blasphemy. Jesus' execution is, in significant part, for *this* claim. - **Matthew 24:30** — *"They will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and gr

to.title: Pierced Side Blood And Water
to.body: # Pierced Side: Blood and Water ## NT Text — John 19:34-37 A soldier pierces Jesus' side; blood and water flow. John cites Zechariah 12:10. ## OT Antecedents - **Zech 12:10** — They will look on him whom they pierced. - **Exod 17:6** — Water from struck rock. - **Ezek 47:1** — Water from temple. ## Shared structure - Pierced side - Blood and water - Look-and-mourn - Temple-water echo ## Theological motif From the smitten Rock flow blood (atonement) and water (purification/Spirit). ## Counterarguments 1. **Medical detail?** *John insists on witness to real death.* 2. **Ezekiel-temple link plausible?** *John 7:38 anchors it.* ## Embedding hint A pierced side issuing blood and water as fulfillment of Zech 12 and temple-river imagery.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ἐκκεντέω' (they have pierced) links 'Pierced Side Blood And Water' and 'Coming On Clouds' through creation.

from.title: Pierced Side Blood And Water
from.body: # Pierced Side: Blood and Water ## NT Text — John 19:34-37 A soldier pierces Jesus' side; blood and water flow. John cites Zechariah 12:10. ## OT Antecedents - **Zech 12:10** — They will look on him whom they pierced. - **Exod 17:6** — Water from struck rock. - **Ezek 47:1** — Water from temple. ## Shared structure - Pierced side - Blood and water - Look-and-mourn - Temple-water echo ## Theological motif From the smitten Rock flow blood (atonement) and water (purification/Spirit). ## Counterarguments 1. **Medical detail?** *John insists on witness to real death.* 2. **Ezekiel-temple link plausible?** *John 7:38 anchors it.* ## Embedding hint A pierced side issuing blood and water as fulfillment of Zech 12 and temple-river imagery.

to.title: Coming On Clouds
to.body: # Coming on the Clouds ## The appearance The Son of Man comes on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory; every eye will see him, and those who pierced him will mourn. ## Mode / context Promised at the ascension (Acts 1:11) and across NT eschatology. ## Significance The eschatological theophany — visible, universal, vindicating, judging. ## Connects to `[[../Titles/Son-Of-Man]]` ## Embedding hint The future global-visible theophany — cloud-vehicle returning in glory.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'σείω' (was stirred) appears in both 'Haggai Glory Of Latter House' and 'Cosmic Signs' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Haggai Glory Of Latter House
from.body: # Glory of the Latter House → Christ Enters the Second Temple ## OT Story — Haggai 2:6-9 'Yet once more I will shake the heavens and the earth… and the desire of all nations shall come, and I will fill this house with glory… The glory of this latter house shall be greater than the former.' ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 12:26-29: Cited as future shaking, leaving only the unshakable. - Luke 21:5-6: The temple's stones will fall. - John 2:13-22: Jesus' presence as the temple-glory. ## Shared structure - Cosmic shaking. - Desire of nations coming. - Latter-house glory surpassing. - Christ in the temple as glory's arrival. ## Theological motif Christ as the glory of the latter house. ## Counterarguments 1. **'Desire of nations' is variously translated.** *Even minimal readings carry messianic horizon; canon converges.* 2. **Second temple was destroyed.** *Yes — its glory was Christ entering, not the building.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about shaking heavens and earth, desire of nations, or greater glory of the latter house.

to.title: Cosmic Signs
to.body: # Cosmic Signs ## The oracle Before the Day of YHWH, wonders appear in heaven and on earth — sun darkened, moon to blood, stars falling, heavens rolled like a scroll. ## Date / context Recurring prophetic topos from Isaiah through Joel into apocalyptic. ## Fulfillment Jesus appropriates the imagery for his return (Matthew 24:29); Revelation 6 weaves it into the sixth seal. Initial fulfillment may attach to Pentecost-darkness (Acts 2:19-20) and AD 70. ## Connects to (general cosmic motif — no narrower stems) ## Embedding hint Decreation language — the heavens unraveling as the signature of divine intervention.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'συμπληρόω' (they were being swamped) appears in both 'Pentecost Tongues' and 'Pentecost Fire And Wind' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Pentecost Tongues
from.body: # Pentecost Tongues ## What happens Fifty days after Passover, the Spirit descends on the gathered disciples with sound of wind and tongues of fire; they proclaim God's mighty works in the many languages of the nations gathered in Jerusalem. ## Typological significance Babel reversed — scattered tongues regathered around one gospel. Sinai's fire on the mountain becomes Spirit-fire on each believer, inaugurating the new covenant temple of persons. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Festivals]]` ## Embedding hint Reversed Babel: Spirit-fire unites scattered tongues around one gospel.

to.title: Pentecost Fire And Wind
to.body: # Pentecost — Fire and Wind ## The appearance A sound like a mighty rushing wind fills the house; tongues as of fire appear, distributed and resting on each of them; all are filled with the Holy Spirit and begin to speak in other tongues. ## Mode / context Fiftieth day after Passover, c. AD 30, in Jerusalem. ## Significance The Sinai theophany (fire, wind, voices) reapplied to the church; the new-covenant Spirit indwells all flesh. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint Sinai inverted: instead of one mediator ascending to fire, fire descends on many.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'λέων' (of the lion) links 'Revelation 13 Beast From Sea' and 'Daniel Lions Den' through creation, water.

from.title: Revelation 13 Beast From Sea
from.body: # Revelation 13: Beast from the Sea ## NT Text — Rev 13:1-10 A beast rises from the sea — Daniel 7's four beasts combined into one. Political tyranny absorbs the world's worship. ## OT Antecedents - **Dan 7:2-7** — Four beasts from the sea. - **Ps 74:13-14** — Heads of leviathan crushed. - **Isa 27:1** — Leviathan, the twisting serpent. ## Shared structure - Sea origin - Many heads/horns - Blasphemy - Authority over peoples - War on saints ## Theological motif Daniel-7 tyrant-beasts gathered into one final-form anti-kingdom. ## Counterarguments 1. **Roman empire specifically?** *Initial referent; pattern recurs.* 2. **Mythological leviathan?** *Adopted to depict chaos-beast.* ## Embedding hint A sea-beast tyranny vision concentrating Daniel 7's four beasts into one.

to.title: Daniel Lions Den
to.body: # Daniel in the Lions' Den ## What happens Sealed in a den of lions for praying to YHWH, Daniel emerges unharmed at dawn; the king proclaims YHWH the living God. ## Typological significance Sealed in a stone pit at evening, emerging alive at dawn — a transparent resurrection pattern. The sealed stone, the night vigil, the dawn vindication all anticipate the empty tomb. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs/Death-And-Resurrection]]`, `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint Sealed pit, dawn vindication — exilic resurrection prefigured.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'אֲפֵלָה' (of.gloom); both units touch on bread, judgment, light, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Day Of The LORD Joel
from.body: # Day of the LORD (Joel) ## The oracle The day of YHWH is great and very awesome — darkness, locust-army, cosmic upheaval — yet whoever calls on the name of YHWH shall be saved, and his Spirit will be poured out on all flesh. ## Date / context Joel, dating disputed (9th-5th c. BC), prompted by a devastating locust plague read as eschatological harbinger. ## Fulfillment Peter applies Joel 2:28-32 to Pentecost (Acts 2:16-21); Paul cites "everyone who calls" of Christ (Romans 10:13). Final fulfillment awaits the consummation. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Mount-Zion]]`, `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint Day of YHWH as both judgment-darkness and Spirit-effusion — terror and mercy in one event.

to.title: Day Of The LORD Zephaniah
to.body: # Day of the LORD (Zephaniah) ## The oracle "The great day of YHWH is near… a day of wrath, a day of distress and anguish, a day of ruin and devastation, a day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness." ## Date / context Zephaniah under Josiah, late 7th c.
