Psalm 40 is the controlling text. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 40:6-8** — Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — a body you prepared. - **Isa 1:11** — I have had enough of burnt offerings. - **1 Sam 15:22** — To obey is better than sacrifice. ## Shared structure - Repetition signals insufficiency - Body prepared - I have come to do your will - Single offering - Perfection in those sanctified ## Theological motif Psalm 40's prophetic obedience model is fulfilled in incarnation-obedience-substitution. ## Counterarguments 1. **Did OT sacrifices forgive?** *Yes — by anticipating Christ; their efficacy was borrowed.* 2. **Antinomian danger?** *Holiness is the goal, not its repudiation.* ## Embedding hint A single-offering finality argument citing Psalm 40's body-prepared obedience.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word '[is].wrapped' (freq 3) is peripheral; theme overlap: creation_new, spirit_breath.

from.title: Elijah Cave Whisper
from.body: # Elijah at the Cave / Still Small Voice → God's Presence in Quiet ## OT Story — 1 Kings 19:9-18 Fleeing Jezebel, Elijah comes to Horeb. Wind, earthquake, fire pass — the LORD is not in them. Then a still small voice. Elijah covers his face. ## NT Fulfillment - John 14:27: My peace I give to you, not as the world gives. - 1 Kings 19:12: A low whisper. - Zechariah 4:6: Not by might, not by power, but by my Spirit. ## Shared structure - Theophany not in spectacle but in whisper. - Prophet's despair met with gentle disclosure. - Re-commissioning after low point. - Pattern: incarnation rather than thunderbolt. ## Theological motif Quiet presence — God revealed gently. ## Counterarguments 1. **Carmel fire vs. cave whisper contradict.** *No — both are God's voice, suited to the moment.* 2. **'Still small voice' is over-quoted.** *Its theological grammar is foundational nonetheless.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about gentle whisper, God not in earthquake/fire, or peace not as the world gives.

to.title: Isaiah 25 Banquet On Mountain
to.body: # Banquet on the Mountain / Death Swallowed Up → Marriage Supper ## OT Story — Isaiah 25:6-9 On this mountain the LORD will make a feast of rich food for all peoples; he will swallow up death forever and wipe away tears. ## NT Fulfillment - Matthew 8:11: Many will come from east and west to recline with Abraham. - Revelation 19:9: Blessed are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb. - 1 Corinthians 15:54: Death is swallowed up in victory. ## Shared structure - Mountain-feast for all nations. - Death swallowed (cosmic reversal). - Tears wiped away. - Eschatological banquet pattern. ## Theological motif Eschatological banquet — death undone. ## Counterarguments 1. **Israel-only feast.** *'All peoples' is in the very text; canon expands explicitly.* 2. **Tears-wiping is Revelation's image.** *Revelation deliberately echoes Isaiah 25.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a banquet on the mountain, death swallowed, or tears wiped away.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'keys' (freq 6) is peripheral; theme overlap: creation_new, judgment.

from.title: Revelation 1 Son Of Man Vision
from.body: # Revelation 1: Son of Man Vision ## NT Text — Rev 1:12-18 John sees one 'like a son of man' among golden lampstands — eyes like fire, feet like burnished bronze, voice like many waters. Daniel 7 and 10 are gathered. ## OT Antecedents - **Dan 7:13** — One like a son of man. - **Dan 10:5-6** — Man clothed in linen, eyes like fire. - **Ezek 1:7** — Feet like burnished bronze. ## Shared structure - Among lampstands - Son-of-Man title - Daniel-10 imagery - Voice like waters - Sword from mouth ## Theological motif Son-Of-Man's heavenly enthronement displayed; he walks among the churches. ## Counterarguments 1. **Pure Daniel-collage?** *Yes — Revelation is OT-saturated.* 2. **Theophanic Christ?** *The vision is unmistakably divine in form.* ## Embedding hint A Son-of-Man theophany among lampstands gathering Daniel 7 and 10 imagery.

to.title: Millennial Reign
to.body: # Millennial Reign ## The oracle Satan is bound for a thousand years; the martyrs reign with Christ; after the thousand years Satan is loosed, gathers the nations, and is destroyed. ## Date / context Revelation 20; debated whether literal-future (premillennial), present-symbolic (amillennial), or pre-parousia (postmillennial). ## Fulfillment Interpretation contested across Christian traditions; the text remains. ## Connects to (no narrower stems) ## Embedding hint The most exegetically disputed eschatological text — a thousand-year interregnum.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ὁσάκις' (as often as); both units touch on blood, resurrection, sacrifice, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Revelation 11 Two Witnesses
from.body: # Revelation 11: Two Witnesses ## NT Text — Rev 11:3-13 Two witnesses prophesy in sackcloth, with power to shut up the heavens and turn water to blood — Elijah and Moses echoes. Slain, then raised after three and a half days. ## OT Antecedents - **1 Kgs 17:1** — Elijah shuts up the heavens. - **Exod 7:17-20** — Moses turns water to blood. - **Zech 4** — Two olive trees and lampstands. ## Shared structure - Two witnesses - Sackcloth prophecy - Elijah-Moses signs - Death and resurrection - Vindication ## Theological motif Elijah-Moses typology continues in church's two-pronged witness; resurrection of the witnesses prefigures the saints. ## Counterarguments 1. **Identifiable individuals?** *Pattern more than persons; cessation impossible.* 2. **Three-and-a-half days?** *Limited tribulation, dependent on Daniel's 'time, times, half a time.'* ## Embedding hint A two-witnesses ministry-and-resurrection echoing Elijah-Moses signs and Zechariah 4.

to.title: Lords Supper
to.body: # Lord's Supper ## What it is The new-covenant memorial meal instituted by Jesus at the last Passover, in which bread and cup signify his body given and blood shed, proclaiming his death until he comes. ## Key elements - Bread broken — "this is my body" - Cup — "the new covenant in my blood" - Memorial: "do this in remembrance of me" - Proclamation of the Lord's death - Anticipation of the marriage feast ## NT fulfillment Paul gives the supper's words as direct dominical tradition (1 Cor 11:23-26), framing it as the church's covenantal renewal until the parousia. It draws together Passover's lamb, Sinai's blood ratification, Jeremiah's new covenant, and Isaiah's eschatological banquet into a single rite. ## Connects to `[[../Festivals/Passover]]`, `[[../Festivals/Marriage-Feast]]`, `[[../Covenants/New-Covenant]]`, `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Symbols/Lamb]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint The instituted new-covenant meal of bread and cup — uniquely the only festival Jesus himself inaugurates, semantically the synthesis-rite of Passover, covenant, and eschatological banquet.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'witness' (freq 5) is central to both; theme overlap: covenant.

from.title: Covenant At Shechem
from.body: # Covenant at Shechem ## What it is Joshua's farewell covenant renewal where Israel, gathered between Ebal and Gerizim, recounts God's faithfulness and pledges exclusive allegiance to YHWH, erecting a witness stone under the oak. ## Key elements - Historical prologue rehearsing God's acts - Call to choose this day whom you will serve - Renouncing foreign gods - Witness stone set up under the oak - Written in the book of the law of God ## NT fulfillment Stephen recalls Shechem as one of the patriarchal burial sites within Israel's covenant memory (Acts 7:15-16). Hebrews contrasts the speaking blood of Christ at the heavenly Zion with every prior covenant gathering, including Shechem (Heb 12:24-25). ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Joshua]]`, `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/Josiah-Renewal-Covenant]]` ## Embedding hint The land-entry covenant renewal at Shechem — distinct as the threshold renewal between conquest and judgeship, framed by the choose-this-day decision motif.

to.title: Jacob Laban Covenant
to.body: # Jacob-Laban Covenant ## What it is A boundary covenant between Jacob and Laban at Galeed/Mizpah, marked by a stone heap and pillar, in which each swears not to cross over to harm the other and invokes God as witness. ## Key elements - Heap of stones and standing pillar as witness - Sacrificial meal sealing the agreement - Mutual oath of non-aggression - Mizpah invocation — "The LORD watch between you and me" - God of Abraham and God of Nahor as judges ## NT fulfillment No direct NT citation, but it stands as a paradigmatic boundary covenant and a witness to how the patriarchal God arbitrates between estranged parties — anticipating the gospel ministry of reconciliation (2 Cor 5). ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Jacob]]`, `[[../Covenants/Abimelech-Isaac-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/Blood-Covenant]]` ## Embedding hint The boundary-truce covenant with standing stones — distinct as a non-aggression treaty between covenantally adjacent parties rather than between God and a people.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'συντρίβω' (bruised) appears in both 'Bones Not Broken' and 'Pierced One Zechariah' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Bones Not Broken
from.body: # Bones Not Broken ## The oracle "He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken" (Psalm 34:20), echoing the Passover statute that no bone of the lamb be broken (Exodus 12:46). ## Date / context Davidic psalm and mosaic Passover regulation, joined typologically. ## Fulfillment John records that the soldiers did not break Jesus' legs because he was already dead, and cites the verse explicitly (John 19:33-36). ## Connects to `[[../Places/Calvary]]`, `[[../Titles/Lamb-Of-God]]`, `[[../Symbols/Passover-Lamb]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint A liturgical detail of the Passover lamb fulfilled in crucifixion mechanics.

to.title: Pierced One Zechariah
to.body: # Pierced One (Zechariah 12:10) ## The oracle YHWH pours out a spirit of grace and supplication on Jerusalem so that they look on "me whom they have pierced" and mourn for him as for an only son. ## Date / context Post-exilic oracle of Zechariah, late 6th c. BC, looking to a future day of repentance. ## Fulfillment John applies it to the spear-thrust at the cross (John 19:34-37); Revelation 1:7 uses it of the second coming, when all tribes will mourn. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]` ## Embedding hint YHWH speaks as the one pierced — the identity of the suffering one with God himself.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'λουτρόν' (washing) appears in both 'Bride Of Christ Eschatological' and 'New Heart Ezekiel 36' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Bride Of Christ Eschatological
from.body: # Bride of Christ (Eschatological) ## The oracle The marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready; the New Jerusalem comes down "as a bride adorned for her husband." ## Date / context Revelation's climactic visions; rooted in the OT marriage metaphor for YHWH and Israel. ## Fulfillment Awaits the consummation; the church already betrothed (2 Cor 11:2) lives toward the wedding. ## Connects to (no narrower stems) ## Embedding hint The eschatological wedding — covenantal union completed in glory.

to.title: New Heart Ezekiel 36
to.body: # New Heart (Ezekiel 36) ## The oracle "I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean… A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you… and I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes." ## Date / context Ezekiel's restoration oracle to the exiles. ## Fulfillment Jesus assumes it as common knowledge with Nicodemus ("born of water and Spirit," John 3:5); Paul echoes the washing-of-regeneration language (Titus 3:5). ## Connects to (no narrower stems) ## Embedding hint The triad of cleansing, new heart, and indwelling Spirit — the anatomy of regeneration.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἐκδέχομαι' (awaiting); both units touch on bread, messiah, resurrection, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Athens Unknown God Sermon
from.body: # Athens: Unknown God Sermon ## NT Text — Acts 17:16-34 Paul preaches at the Areopagus, taking the 'unknown god' altar as bridge. He proclaims the Creator who has appointed a day of judgment via the risen Christ. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 1** — God who made the world. - **Isa 42:5** — God who gives breath to all. - **Ps 96:13** — He comes to judge the earth. ## Shared structure - Cultural bridge - Creator proclamation - Repentance summons - Resurrection-judgment climax - Mixed response ## Theological motif Mars Hill modeling of natural-revelation-to-gospel-proclamation flow. ## Counterarguments 1. **Did Paul soften the gospel?** *No — resurrection-judgment is the climax.* 2. **Was the sermon a failure?** *Some believed (Dionysius, Damaris).* ## Embedding hint An Areopagus address bridging pagan altars to Creator-and-Judge through resurrection.

to.title: Two Goats Yom Kippur
to.body: # Two Goats of Atonement → Christ's Death and Bearing-Away ## OT Story — Leviticus 16:7-22 On Yom Kippur, two goats: one slaughtered as sin offering, its blood taken behind the veil; the other (the scapegoat) bears confessed sins into the wilderness, never to return. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 9:11-14: Christ entered the holy places by his own blood. - Hebrews 10:11-14: One offering for sins forever. - John 1:29: The Lamb of God who takes away (bears away) the sin of the world. ## Shared structure - Two complementary aspects of one atonement. - Blood applied / sin removed. - Wilderness as place of expulsion. - Annual repetition replaced by once-for-all. ## Theological motif Substitution and removal in one event at the cross. ## Counterarguments 1. **Two goats = two distinct theologies.** *They are commanded as a unity, fulfilled in one person.* 2. **Azazel-goat may be a demon-tribute.** *Even if so, the NT redirects: sin sent back where it came from.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about two goats, blood within / sins removed, or Christ bearing away sin.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἁμάρτημα' (sins) appears in both 'Mercy Seat Atonement' and 'hilasmos' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Mercy Seat Atonement
from.body: # Mercy Seat / Hilasterion → Christ Our Propitiation ## OT Story — Exodus 25:17-22; Leviticus 16:14-15 Over the ark sits the kapporet, the mercy seat, flanked by two cherubim. Once a year the high priest sprinkles blood there for the sins of the people. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 3:25: God put forward Christ as a propitiation (hilasterion). - Hebrews 9:5: The cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat. - 1 John 2:2: He is the propitiation for our sins. ## Shared structure - Blood applied at the place of God's throne. - Two cherubim, glory between (cf. John 20:12 at tomb). - Once-a-year access becomes once-for-all in Christ. - Throne of judgment becomes throne of mercy. ## Theological motif Propitiation — wrath turned aside by blood at the throne. ## Counterarguments 1. **'Hilasterion' may mean 'expiation,' not 'propitiation.'** *Both senses are present; the LXX uses it of the kapporet, anchoring propitiation.* 2. **Two cherubim and tomb-angels is too thin.** *John 20:12 deliberately echoes the mercy seat in resurrection language.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about blood at the mercy seat, propitiation, or two angels at the place of presence.

to.title: hilasmos
to.body: # hilasmos (ἱλασμός) — propitiation ## Semantic field The means of turning aside wrath or wiping away sin (debate: propitiation vs expiation). Related noun *hilasterion* names the mercy seat / atonement cover. ## Theological weight "He is the hilasmos for our sins" (1 John 2:2). Christ is both the priest who offers and the offering that turns aside divine wrath while expiating sin's defilement. ## Septuagint mapping Renders Hebrew kapparah / kipper. ## Embedding hint Atoning sacrifice that addresses both God's wrath and humanity's defilement.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'φλόξ' (flame) appears in both 'Burning Bush' and 'Patmos Christ Vision Revelation 1' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Burning Bush
from.body: # Burning Bush ## The appearance The angel of YHWH appears to Moses in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush that burns yet is not consumed; God speaks from the bush, calls Moses by name, reveals the name "I AM," and commissions him. ## Mode / context Far side of the wilderness, at Horeb, the mountain of God. ## Significance Disclosure of the divine name (YHWH); commission of the deliverer; ground holy. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Places/Sinai]]`, `[[../Places/Wilderness]]` ## Embedding hint The unconsumed flame as image of divine presence — fire that does not destroy.

to.title: Patmos Christ Vision Revelation 1
to.body: # Patmos Christ-Vision ## The appearance On the Lord's Day on Patmos, John hears a loud voice and turns to see one like a son of man amid seven golden lampstands — robed and golden-sashed, hair white as wool, eyes like fire, feet like burnished bronze, voice like many waters, holding seven stars, with a sharp two-edged sword from his mouth. ## Mode / context Exilic vision under Domitian, late 1st c. ## Significance The risen Christ given the full attributes of the Ancient of Days; the church's Lord seen in cosmic glory. ## Connects to `[[../Titles/Son-Of-Man]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint The composite-Christophany — Danielic Ancient-of-Days imagery transferred wholesale to the risen Son.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'they have pierced' (ἐκκεντέω); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and seed worth surfacing.

from.title: Joseph Reveals Self
from.body: # Joseph Reveals Himself → Israel Recognizes the Pierced One ## OT Story — Genesis 45:1-15 After testing his brothers, Joseph dismisses his attendants and weeps aloud: 'I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?' The brothers are terrified, then reconciled. He sends them back to bring their father. ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 7:13: Joseph 'made himself known to his brothers' — Stephen uses the verb as type of Christ revealed to Israel. - Zechariah 12:10: They will look on him whom they have pierced and mourn. - Romans 11:25-27: All Israel will be saved when the full number of Gentiles comes in. ## Shared structure - Rejected brother becomes ruler of the world. - Self-revelation precedes reconciliation. - Tears, terror, then peace. - Reunion with the father follows. ## Theological motif Recognition of the rejected one as savior. ## Counterarguments 1. **This reads NT eschatology back too eagerly.** *Acts 7:13 makes the type explicit; Stephen's own martyr-speech demands it.* 2. **Brothers' fear is just political.** *The text frames it as moral — 'God has found out your iniquity' (Gen 44:16).* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a rejected savior revealing himself to his own and weeping reconciliation.

to.title: Pierced Side Blood And Water
to.body: # Pierced Side: Blood and Water ## NT Text — John 19:34-37 A soldier pierces Jesus' side; blood and water flow. John cites Zechariah 12:10. ## OT Antecedents - **Zech 12:10** — They will look on him whom they pierced. - **Exod 17:6** — Water from struck rock. - **Ezek 47:1** — Water from temple. ## Shared structure - Pierced side - Blood and water - Look-and-mourn - Temple-water echo ## Theological motif From the smitten Rock flow blood (atonement) and water (purification/Spirit). ## Counterarguments 1. **Medical detail?** *John insists on witness to real death.* 2. **Ezekiel-temple link plausible?** *John 7:38 anchors it.* ## Embedding hint A pierced side issuing blood and water as fulfillment of Zech 12 and temple-river imagery.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'κρύσταλλος' (crystal) appears in both 'Ezekiel 1 Throne Chariot' and 'Ezekiel 47 River From Temple' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Ezekiel 1 Throne Chariot
from.body: # Throne Chariot / Merkavah → Revelation's Throne Vision ## OT Story — Ezekiel 1 Ezekiel sees a stormy theophany — four living creatures, wheels within wheels, a throne of sapphire, a figure of fire and rainbow. The glory of the LORD. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 4:6-8: Four living creatures around the throne, full of eyes. - Revelation 4:3: Around the throne a rainbow. - Isaiah 6:1-3: Throne with seraphim crying 'Holy.' ## Shared structure - Throne mobile — God present in exile. - Four creatures, eyes, wheels. - Fire and rainbow encircling. - Voice from the throne. ## Theological motif Throne theology of presence in exile. ## Counterarguments 1. **Ezekiel's vision is unique.** *Revelation deliberately rebuilds it for Christ-on-throne.* 2. **Merkavah mysticism is speculative.** *Revelation's canonical rereading is not speculative.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a throne with four living creatures, wheels of glory, or rainbow around the throne.

to.title: Ezekiel 47 River From Temple
to.body: # River from the Temple → River of Life From the Throne ## OT Story — Ezekiel 47:1-12 Water flows from under the temple threshold — ankle, knee, waist, swimming-deep. It heals the Dead Sea. Trees on its banks bear fruit each month; their leaves heal. ## NT Fulfillment - John 7:37-39: Rivers of living water from the believer — Spirit. - Revelation 22:1-2: River of the water of life from the throne, tree with leaves for healing of nations. - John 4:14: A spring welling up to eternal life. ## Shared structure - River from the sanctuary's threshold. - Increasing depth — abundance. - Dead made alive (Dead Sea healed). - Trees of life with monthly fruit, healing leaves. ## Theological motif River of life flowing from the Christ-temple. ## Counterarguments 1. **Temple-water in Ezekiel is literal architecture.** *Revelation 22 spiritualizes the same river canonically.* 2. **Healing-leaves is poetic.** *Same Greek (Rev 22:2) for nations' healing.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about water from the temple, river of life, or healing leaves.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'גְּזֵרָה' (infertile) appears in both 'Two Goats Yom Kippur' and 'nasa' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Two Goats Yom Kippur
from.body: # Two Goats of Atonement → Christ's Death and Bearing-Away ## OT Story — Leviticus 16:7-22 On Yom Kippur, two goats: one slaughtered as sin offering, its blood taken behind the veil; the other (the scapegoat) bears confessed sins into the wilderness, never to return. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 9:11-14: Christ entered the holy places by his own blood. - Hebrews 10:11-14: One offering for sins forever. - John 1:29: The Lamb of God who takes away (bears away) the sin of the world. ## Shared structure - Two complementary aspects of one atonement. - Blood applied / sin removed. - Wilderness as place of expulsion. - Annual repetition replaced by once-for-all. ## Theological motif Substitution and removal in one event at the cross. ## Counterarguments 1. **Two goats = two distinct theologies.** *They are commanded as a unity, fulfilled in one person.* 2. **Azazel-goat may be a demon-tribute.** *Even if so, the NT redirects: sin sent back where it came from.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about two goats, blood within / sins removed, or Christ bearing away sin.

to.title: nasa
to.body: # nasa (נָשָׂא) — lift / bear / forgive ## Semantic field To lift up, carry, bear — physical lifting, but extended to bearing sin (in judgment or vicarious atonement) and forgiving (lifting sin off). ## Theological weight The scapegoat *nasa* the sins of Israel to the wilderness (Lev 16:22). The Servant *nasa* the sins of many (Isa 53). To "lift face" can mean show favor; to "lift sin" can mean forgive. ## Septuagint mapping Various — frequently `airo` (take/lift) or `aphiemi` (forgive). ## Embedding hint Lift/carry/bear — vehicle of vicarious sin-bearing and forgiveness.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'the same way' (freq 2) is peripheral; theme overlap: priesthood, creation_new.

from.title: Aarons Breastplate Twelve Stones
from.body: # Aaron's Breastplate of Twelve Stones → Christ Carries His People on His Heart ## OT Story — Exodus 28:15-30 The high priest's breastplate bears twelve gemstones, each engraved with a tribe's name, worn over his heart whenever he enters the holy place. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 4:14-16: A great high priest who sympathizes — boldness to draw near. - Hebrews 7:25: He always lives to intercede. - Revelation 21:19-20: New Jerusalem's foundations are twelve precious stones. ## Shared structure - Tribes carried on the priest's heart into God's presence. - Twelve named individuals as foundation gemstones. - Intercession by representation. - From breastplate to city wall — the people endure in the priest's memorial. ## Theological motif High-priestly intercession by name. ## Counterarguments 1. **Stones are decorative.** *Their named engraving and 'memorial before the LORD' (Ex 28:29) make them theological.* 2. **Revelation's stones differ.** *Differences signal new-creation transformation, not negation, of the breastplate.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about names worn over the heart, twelve gemstones, or representative intercession.

to.title: Covenant Of Levi
to.body: # Covenant of Levi ## What it is God's covenant with the tribe of Levi for life and peace, charging the priests to fear God, give true instruction, and guard knowledge, with Malachi indicting their corruption of this covenant. ## Key elements - Granted in exchange for fear and reverence - True torah in their mouth - No iniquity on their lips - Turning many from iniquity - Mediator role between God and people ## NT fulfillment Hebrews argues the Levitical priesthood could not bring perfection and is supplanted by Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek (Heb 7:11-19), so the corrupted covenant Malachi mourns is replaced by a flawless mediator. ## Connects to `[[../Covenants/Priestly-Covenant-Phinehas]]`, `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/New-Covenant]]` ## Embedding hint The tribal-priestly teaching covenant — distinct from Phinehas in emphasizing instructional fidelity rather than zealous atonement.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἦχος (I)' (the report) appears in both 'Babel Reversed At Pentecost' and 'Glory On Sinai' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Babel Reversed At Pentecost
from.body: # Babel Scattered → Pentecost Gathered ## OT Story — Genesis 11:1-9 Humanity, with one language, builds a tower to make a name for itself in defiance of the command to fill the earth. God descends, confuses speech, and scatters the nations. ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 2:1-13: The Spirit descends; every nation hears in its own tongue. - Revelation 7:9-10: A multitude from every tribe and tongue worships the Lamb. - Genesis 12 immediately follows Babel: Abram is called so that in him all nations will be blessed. ## Shared structure - Divine descent in both stories — judgment at Babel, blessing at Pentecost. - Language as the medium: confusion vs. comprehension. - Scattering vs. ingathering of the nations. - A name made for self vs. the Name given by God. ## Theological motif Gospel-In-Narrative — the church reverses Babel without erasing nations. ## Counterarguments 1. **Pentecost is a one-off, not a reversal.** *The Lukan structure (descent, languages, all nations) deliberately echoes and inverts Genesis 11.* 2. **Languages remained distinct after Pentecost.** *Yes — comprehension, not uniformity, is the gift; cf. Rev 7:9 plurality preserved.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about confused vs. understood tongues, scattering vs. gathering nations, or making a name.

to.title: Glory On Sinai
to.body: # Glory on Sinai ## The appearance The mountain is wrapped in thick cloud, thunder, lightning, and trumpet blast; YHWH descends in fire, the mountain quakes, and Moses ascends into the cloud where the glory dwells; later YHWH passes by, proclaiming his name. ## Mode / context Covenant-making at Sinai; the constitutive theophany of Israel. ## Significance The encounter that founds the covenant and the law; pattern of all subsequent glory-theophanies. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Places/Sinai]]` ## Embedding hint The fullest OT theophany — sound, fire, smoke, and name — covenantal in form.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'light' (freq 2) is peripheral; theme overlap: kingship, creation_new.

from.title: Ezekiel Apocrypha Themes
from.body: # 4 Ezra / Apocalyptic Hope → New Creation Theology Backdrop ## OT Story — 2 Esdras (4 Ezra) 7 4 Ezra wrestles with theodicy, exile, and resurrection — featuring the Messiah's reign, new age dawning, and consolation of the righteous. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 8:18-25: Sufferings of this present time vs. coming glory. - 2 Corinthians 4:17: Light affliction working eternal glory. - Revelation 21:1-4: New creation. ## Shared structure - Theodicy in exile. - Two-age framework. - Coming Messiah and consolation. - Resurrection of the righteous. ## Theological motif Two-age apocalyptic backdrop the NT inhabits. ## Counterarguments 1. **Non-canonical apocalypse.** *Yes — but its categories shape NT writers' grammar.* 2. **Risk of conflation.** *The seed-bank flags it precisely so NT readers can distinguish source from canon.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about two ages, coming Messiah, or theodicy resolved by resurrection.

to.title: Sabbath Covenant
to.body: # Sabbath Covenant ## What it is The perpetual sign-covenant of weekly Sabbath rest, marking Israel as a people sanctified by YHWH and rooted in the rhythm of creation. ## Key elements - Sign between God and Israel forever - Imitatio Dei — six days work, seventh rest - Capital seriousness of Sabbath violation - Tied to creation (Ex 31) and exodus (Deut 5) - Sanctification marker ## NT fulfillment Hebrews 4 presents a remaining Sabbath rest for the people of God, entered through faith in Christ who invites the weary to true rest (Matt 11:28-30). Paul treats the weekly Sabbath as a shadow whose substance belongs to Christ (Col 2:16-17). ## Connects to `[[../Festivals/Sabbath]]`, `[[../Festivals/Sabbatical-Year]]`, `[[../Festivals/Jubilee]]`, `[[../Numbers/Seven]]` ## Embedding hint The weekly sign-covenant of rest — distinct as a covenant inscribed in time rather than place or person, oscillating with creation's heptadic rhythm.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'דְּמָמָה' (of.a.whisper) appears in both 'Calming Of Storm' and 'Still Small Voice Elijah' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Calming Of Storm
from.body: # Calming of the Storm ## What happens Crossing the Sea of Galilee, Jesus sleeps in the stern through a violent storm; rebuked, the wind and sea fall silent. The disciples ask, 'Who then is this?' ## Typological significance Only YHWH stills the sea in the Psalms — Jesus' authority over chaos discloses His divine identity. The sleep-and-rise of the Lord in the boat patterns His death and resurrection over the chaos of the abyss. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Galilee]]` ## Embedding hint Sleeping Lord rises to silence the deep — Psalm 107 enacted in person.

to.title: Still Small Voice Elijah
to.body: # Still Small Voice at Horeb ## The appearance At Horeb, a great wind, then an earthquake, then a fire pass by — but YHWH is not in them; after the fire, "a sound of sheer silence" (or still small voice), and Elijah wraps his face in his mantle. ## Mode / context Elijah's flight after Carmel and Jezebel's threat; same mountain as Sinai. ## Significance Counter-Sinai: God reveals himself not in dramatic phenomena but in low whisper; recommissioning of a despairing prophet. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Elijah]]`, `[[../Places/Sinai]]`, `[[../Places/Wilderness]]` ## Embedding hint The minimalist theophany — God in the silence after the spectacle.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'σάκκος' (sackcloth) appears in both 'Moon Turned To Blood' and 'Time Times Half Time' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Moon Turned To Blood
from.body: # Moon Turned to Blood ## The oracle "The moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of YHWH comes." ## Date / context Joel's oracle, cited at Pentecost and in Revelation. ## Fulfillment Peter cites it as part of the inaugurated last-days outpouring (Acts 2:20); Revelation 6:12 deploys it at the sixth seal. ## Connects to `[[../Symbols/Blood]]` ## Embedding hint The lunar half of the sun-and-moon eschatological-sign pair.

to.title: Time Times Half Time
to.body: # Time, Times, and Half a Time ## The oracle The persecution of the saints is limited to "a time, times, and half a time" — a fixed and bounded period of tribulation. ## Date / context Danielic visions, 6th c. BC. ## Fulfillment Revelation reuses the formula (12:14) alongside parallel measures (forty-two months, 1260 days) to describe the church's wilderness period under satanic assault. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint The bounded-tribulation motif — evil's permission has a fixed expiration.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'of desolation' (freq 3) is peripheral; theme overlap: temple, spirit_breath.

from.title: Falling Of Jerusalem
from.body: # Falling of Jerusalem ## The oracle Jesus weeps over Jerusalem and predicts that not one stone of the temple will be left on another; the city will be surrounded by armies and trampled by the Gentiles until their times are fulfilled. ## Date / context Passion week and earlier oracles in Luke, c. AD 30. ## Fulfillment Fulfilled in literal detail by Titus' siege in AD 70 — temple razed, city destroyed. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint The most precisely fulfilled NT prophecy of a near-horizon event.

to.title: Seventy Weeks Daniel 9
to.body: # Seventy Weeks (Daniel 9) ## The oracle Seventy weeks are decreed upon Daniel's people to finish transgression, atone for iniquity, bring everlasting righteousness, seal vision, and anoint a most holy place; after sixty-nine weeks Messiah will be cut off. ## Date / context Given to Daniel in Babylon, c. 539 BC, during his prayer over Jeremiah's seventy-year prophecy. ## Fulfillment Christian interpretation sees Messiah's "cutting off" in Christ's crucifixion (c. AD 30) and the destruction of the temple in AD 70; some read the seventieth week eschatologically. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]`, `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Numbers/Seventy]]`, `[[../Numbers/Seven]]` ## Embedding hint The most quantitatively specific messianic timeline in the OT.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἔκτρωμα' (untimely birth) appears in both 'Damascus Road Christ' and 'Resurrection Appearances' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Damascus Road Christ
from.body: # Damascus Road Christophany ## The appearance At midday, a light brighter than the sun shines around Saul; he falls to the ground and hears, "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?… I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting." He is blinded for three days. ## Mode / context Saul's journey to arrest Christians in Damascus, c. AD 33-35. ## Significance Post-ascension theophany of the risen Christ; commissioning of the apostle to the Gentiles. ## Connects to `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint The post-ascension Christophany — light, voice, conversion-commission of a persecutor.

to.title: Resurrection Appearances
to.body: # Resurrection Appearances ## The appearance The risen Jesus appears to Mary Magdalene, the women, Peter, the two on the Emmaus road, the disciples in the upper room (with and without Thomas), seven by the Sea of Galilee, the eleven on a mountain, and to more than five hundred at once. ## Mode / context Forty days between resurrection and ascension. ## Significance Bodily theophanies of the new-creation Lord — recognizable yet glorified, eating and being touched yet passing through doors. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Places/Galilee]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Motifs/Death-And-Resurrection]]` ## Embedding hint The serial-resurrection theophanies — God-with-us continuing past the cross.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἀντίτυπος' (copies); both units touch on creation, messiah, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Flood Baptism
from.body: # Flood / Ark → Baptism Into Christ ## OT Story — Genesis 6-9 A righteous remnant is preserved in a wooden vessel as God's judgment washes the world clean. Noah's family passes through the waters of death and emerges into a new creation, greeted by a covenant rainbow. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Peter 3:20-21: "Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you" — through-water salvation in Christ's resurrection. - Matthew 24:37-39: The days of Noah typify unwatchful generations before the Son of Man's coming. - 2 Peter 2:5: Noah, "a herald of righteousness," prefigures the gospel preached before judgment. ## Shared structure - Judgment-bearing waters pass over a covenant household. - Wooden vessel as means of rescue (cf. cross, ark of Moses, ark of covenant). - New-creation emergence: dove, olive branch, altar. - Eight souls (1 Pet 3:20) — number of new beginning. ## Theological motif Death-And-Resurrection through water; salvation through judgment, not around it. ## Counterarguments 1. **Baptism is symbolic; the flood was judgment, not salvation.** *Peter's point is precisely that the waters that judged the world saved the family inside the ark — the same dynamic in baptism united to Christ.* 2. **Typology requires authorial intent.** *1 Peter is itself canonical authorial intent, sealing the linkage.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about passing through water unto salvation, judgment on the wicked alongside rescue of a remnant, or new-creation emergence after deluge.

to.title: Job Mediator Daysman
to.body: # Job's Cry for a Daysman → Christ Our Mediator ## OT Story — Job 9:32-35; 16:19-21 Job: 'There is no umpire between us… Even now my witness is in heaven; he who testifies for me is on high. Oh, that one might plead for a man with God!' He longs for a daysman to lay his hand on both. ## NT Fulfillment - 1 Timothy 2:5: One mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. - Hebrews 7:25: He always lives to intercede. - Hebrews 9:24: He has entered heaven itself to appear in God's presence for us. ## Shared structure - Awareness of the gap between God and man. - Cry for an arbiter. - Witness in heaven foreshadowed. - Faith reaching beyond available means. ## Theological motif Mediation — the gospel answer to Job's cry. ## Counterarguments 1. **Job speaks in despair.** *His faith breaks through despair; canon affirms.* 2. **Daysman is poetic, not prophetic.** *Hebrews 9:24 makes the canonical answer explicit.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a mediator between God and man, witness in heaven, or daysman.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'you did wail' (κόπτω); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and prophecy worth surfacing.

from.title: Triumphal Entry Donkey Zechariah
from.body: # Triumphal Entry on a Donkey ## NT Text — Matt 21:1-11; Zech 9:9 Jesus enters Jerusalem riding a donkey, fulfilling Zechariah's oracle of the humble king. Crowds spread cloaks and palms as for a Davidic king, blending Solomon's coronation and the Maccabean palms. ## OT Antecedents - **Zech 9:9** — Your king comes humble, riding on a donkey. - **1 Kgs 1:33-40** — Solomon rides David's mule to his anointing. - **Ps 118:25-26** — Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD — Hosanna. ## Shared structure - King on equid - Crowd acclamation - Coronation imagery - Humility paired with kingship ## Theological motif The Zechariah pattern fuses with the Solomonic to portray Jesus as the humble peace-king. His war-mount is reserved for the eschaton (Rev 19). ## Counterarguments 1. **Did the disciples understand?** *John 12:16 says not until afterward.* 2. **Was the donkey arrangement staged?** *Pre-arranged by Jesus, who controls the symbolism.* ## Embedding hint A messianic entry on a colt fulfilling Zechariah 9:9 with Solomonic and pilgrim-psalm overtones.

to.title: Triumphal Entry On Donkey
to.body: # Triumphal Entry on a Donkey ## The oracle Zion's king comes to her, righteous and having salvation, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt the foal of a donkey, proclaiming peace to the nations. ## Date / context Zechariah, post-exilic Jerusalem, late 6th c. BC. ## Fulfillment Jesus enters Jerusalem on Palm Sunday riding a donkey's colt; the evangelists cite Zechariah explicitly (Matt 21:5; John 12:15). ## Connects to `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]`, `[[../Places/Mount-Zion]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint The humble-king image — peaceable royal advent, not war-horse, but a donkey.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'κρύσταλλος' (crystal); both units touch on kingdom, temple, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Ezekiel 1 Throne Chariot
from.body: # Throne Chariot / Merkavah → Revelation's Throne Vision ## OT Story — Ezekiel 1 Ezekiel sees a stormy theophany — four living creatures, wheels within wheels, a throne of sapphire, a figure of fire and rainbow. The glory of the LORD. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 4:6-8: Four living creatures around the throne, full of eyes. - Revelation 4:3: Around the throne a rainbow. - Isaiah 6:1-3: Throne with seraphim crying 'Holy.' ## Shared structure - Throne mobile — God present in exile. - Four creatures, eyes, wheels. - Fire and rainbow encircling. - Voice from the throne. ## Theological motif Throne theology of presence in exile. ## Counterarguments 1. **Ezekiel's vision is unique.** *Revelation deliberately rebuilds it for Christ-on-throne.* 2. **Merkavah mysticism is speculative.** *Revelation's canonical rereading is not speculative.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a throne with four living creatures, wheels of glory, or rainbow around the throne.

to.title: Revelation 22 River Of Life Tree Of Life
to.body: # Revelation 22: River and Tree of Life ## NT Text — Rev 22:1-5 A river of life flows from the throne; the tree of life bears twelve fruits. Eden's lost garden and Ezekiel's temple-river converge. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 2:9-10** — Eden's tree and river. - **Ezek 47** — River from the temple. - **Joel 3:18** — Fountain from the house of the LORD. ## Shared structure - River from throne - Tree of life - Twelve fruits - Healing leaves - No curse ## Theological motif Eden restored — and surpassed; the curse undone forever. ## Counterarguments 1. **Literal river?** *Symbol-thick — real referent for which symbol points.* 2. **Why twelve fruits?** *Apostolic-tribal completeness for the nations.* ## Embedding hint An Eden-temple-river final vision: tree and river of life in the curse-undone city.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ὁμοιότης' (the same way); both units touch on sacrifice, wisdom, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Covenant Of Levi
from.body: # Covenant of Levi ## What it is God's covenant with the tribe of Levi for life and peace, charging the priests to fear God, give true instruction, and guard knowledge, with Malachi indicting their corruption of this covenant. ## Key elements - Granted in exchange for fear and reverence - True torah in their mouth - No iniquity on their lips - Turning many from iniquity - Mediator role between God and people ## NT fulfillment Hebrews argues the Levitical priesthood could not bring perfection and is supplanted by Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek (Heb 7:11-19), so the corrupted covenant Malachi mourns is replaced by a flawless mediator. ## Connects to `[[../Covenants/Priestly-Covenant-Phinehas]]`, `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/New-Covenant]]` ## Embedding hint The tribal-priestly teaching covenant — distinct from Phinehas in emphasizing instructional fidelity rather than zealous atonement.

to.title: Priest
to.body: # Priest ## What this office is A priest mediates between God and people: offering sacrifice, intercession, and instruction in Torah. The Aaronic priesthood was hereditary, ritually consecrated, and bound to the tabernacle/temple. Priests handled blood, bread, incense, and the holy fire. ## Typological significance / Christ as fulfillment Christ is the great High Priest who offers not the blood of bulls and goats but His own blood, once for all (Hebrews 9-10). His priesthood is after the order of Melchizedek — eternal, royal, and not by lineage but by indestructible life. ## Connects to `[[./High-Priest]]`, `[[./Levitical-Priest]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint Office of God-ward mediation through sacrifice and intercession.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'might be charged' (freq 7) is peripheral; theme overlap: kingship, name_renamed.

from.title: Amos Booth Of David Restored
from.body: # Booth of David Rebuilt → Gentile Inclusion at Jerusalem Council ## OT Story — Amos 9:11-12 'I will rebuild the fallen booth of David… so that they may possess the remnant of Edom and all the nations who are called by my name.' ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 15:13-18: James cites Amos at the council: God has visited the Gentiles. - Romans 15:9-12: Gentiles glorify God for his mercy. - Revelation 5:9: Ransomed for God people from every tribe. ## Shared structure - Davidic tent reopened. - Nations included by the name. - Council uses Amos to validate Gentile mission. - Restoration centered on David's house. ## Theological motif Gentile inclusion grounded in Amos. ## Counterarguments 1. **Amos refers to Israel's restoration only.** *LXX (cited by James) extends to all nations; canonical.* 2. **Council's exegesis is creative.** *It is grounded in LXX text and Spirit-witnessed events.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about rebuilding David's fallen tent or nations called by God's name.

to.title: Enoch Walking With God
to.body: # Enoch Walking With God → Translation and the Coming Lord ## OT Story — Genesis 5:21-24 In a genealogy marked by the refrain "and he died," one name breaks the pattern: Enoch "walked with God, and he was not, for God took him." Seventh from Adam, he is removed from death's tally by direct fellowship with God. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 11:5-6: Enoch was "taken up so that he should not see death" — a paradigm of faith pleasing God. - Jude 14-15: Enoch prophesies the Lord's coming with His holy ones in judgment. - 1 Thessalonians 4:17: Believers alive at the parousia "shall be caught up" — Enoch's pattern universalized. ## Shared structure - Walking with God in an age of corruption. - Bypass of death by divine initiative. - Prophetic anticipation of the Lord's appearing. - Foil to the death-refrain of fallen humanity. ## Theological motif Death-And-Resurrection (anticipated by translation); fellowship as the ground of hope. ## Counterarguments 1. **"He was not" may simply mean he died young.** *Hebrews 11:5 explicitly denies this: he did not see death.* 2. **The Jude prophecy is from 1 Enoch and so non-canonical.** *Jude's inspired citation makes the kernel canonical without endorsing the whole pseudepigraphon.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about walking with God, being taken without dying, or the Lord coming with His holy ones.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'ἦχος (I)' (the report) links 'Ezekiel 43 Glory Returns' and 'Glory On Sinai' through light.

from.title: Ezekiel 43 Glory Returns
from.body: # Glory Returns from the East → Christ Returns; God Dwells With Us ## OT Story — Ezekiel 43:1-9 Ezekiel sees the glory of the LORD coming from the east — the same direction it had departed — and filling the new temple. 'I will dwell in their midst forever.' ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 2:1-4: Spirit fills the house with sound from heaven. - Revelation 21:3: God's dwelling is with man. - Matthew 24:27: As lightning comes from the east, so the Son of Man. ## Shared structure - Glory returns from the same direction it departed. - Filling of new sanctuary. - Promise of permanent dwelling. - Eschatological pattern of return. ## Theological motif Glory returns — culminating dwelling of God with man. ## Counterarguments 1. **Ezekiel's temple is unbuilt.** *Its fulfillment may be Christological and ecclesial-eschatological.* 2. **East-direction is incidental.** *Matthew 24:27 makes the east-direction Christologically deliberate.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about glory returning from the east or God dwelling permanently with his people.

to.title: Glory On Sinai
to.body: # Glory on Sinai ## The appearance The mountain is wrapped in thick cloud, thunder, lightning, and trumpet blast; YHWH descends in fire, the mountain quakes, and Moses ascends into the cloud where the glory dwells; later YHWH passes by, proclaiming his name. ## Mode / context Covenant-making at Sinai; the constitutive theophany of Israel. ## Significance The encounter that founds the covenant and the law; pattern of all subsequent glory-theophanies. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Places/Sinai]]` ## Embedding hint The fullest OT theophany — sound, fire, smoke, and name — covenantal in form.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'blameless' (freq 5) is peripheral; theme overlap: creation_new, spirit_breath.

from.title: Daniel Wise Will Shine
from.body: # Wise Will Shine → Saints Shine Like the Sun ## OT Story — Daniel 12:3 'Those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the firmament, and those who turn many to righteousness, like the stars forever.' ## NT Fulfillment - Matthew 13:43: The righteous will shine like the sun. - Philippians 2:15: Shine as lights in the world. - 1 Corinthians 15:41-42: Star differs from star in glory. ## Shared structure - Wise — those who lead many to righteousness. - Shining as cosmic luminaries. - Eternal radiance. - Discipleship rooted in this hope. ## Theological motif Saints shine as stars in glory. ## Counterarguments 1. **Stars are figurative.** *Resurrection bodies' glory is also figurative-and-real, per 1 Cor 15.* 2. **Wisdom-talk is moralizing.** *Daniel ties it to mass-conversion ministry, not generic morality.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about saints shining like stars or the sun, or turning many to righteousness.

to.title: New Covenant
to.body: # New Covenant ## What it is The prophesied internal covenant in which God writes his law on the heart, gives his Spirit, grants comprehensive forgiveness, and creates a people who all know him directly. ## Key elements - Torah written on the heart - Universal knowledge of God within the community - Definitive forgiveness of sins - Indwelling Spirit (Ezek 36) - Inaugurated by Christ's blood ## NT fulfillment At the last supper Jesus identifies the cup as the new covenant in his blood, ratifying Jeremiah's promise (Luke 22:20). Hebrews argues the old covenant is obsolete because Christ's once-for-all sacrifice secures eternal redemption and full forgiveness (Heb 8-10). ## Connects to `[[../Covenants/Mosaic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Covenants/Abrahamic-Covenant]]`, `[[../Symbols/Blood]]`, `[[../Motifs/Substitutionary-Sacrifice]]`, `[[../Festivals/Lords-Supper]]` ## Embedding hint The interiorized Spirit-mediated covenant of total forgiveness — semantically distinct by its move from external code to inscribed heart.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'ἐλαφρός' (light); both units touch on name, temple, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Job Restoration Doubled
from.body: # Job's Restoration Doubled → Sufferings Compared to Glory ## OT Story — Job 42:10-17 After his trial, the LORD restores Job's fortunes, giving him twice as much. He has seven sons and three daughters, lives 140 years. ## NT Fulfillment - James 5:11: You have seen the steadfastness of Job and the purpose of the Lord. - Romans 8:18: Sufferings are not worth comparing to the glory. - 2 Corinthians 4:17: Light affliction is producing eternal weight of glory. ## Shared structure - Suffering of the righteous followed by surpassing reward. - Doubled inheritance. - Renewed family and life. - Vindication of trust. ## Theological motif Suffering and glory — Christ-shape of resurrection reward. ## Counterarguments 1. **Job's restoration is material.** *James and Romans generalize to spiritual / eschatological glory.* 2. **Children replaced cannot be equal.** *The text emphasizes restoration without trivializing loss.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about restoration after suffering, doubled blessing, or affliction yielding glory.

to.title: Sabbath Covenant
to.body: # Sabbath Covenant ## What it is The perpetual sign-covenant of weekly Sabbath rest, marking Israel as a people sanctified by YHWH and rooted in the rhythm of creation. ## Key elements - Sign between God and Israel forever - Imitatio Dei — six days work, seventh rest - Capital seriousness of Sabbath violation - Tied to creation (Ex 31) and exodus (Deut 5) - Sanctification marker ## NT fulfillment Hebrews 4 presents a remaining Sabbath rest for the people of God, entered through faith in Christ who invites the weary to true rest (Matt 11:28-30). Paul treats the weekly Sabbath as a shadow whose substance belongs to Christ (Col 2:16-17). ## Connects to `[[../Festivals/Sabbath]]`, `[[../Festivals/Sabbatical-Year]]`, `[[../Festivals/Jubilee]]`, `[[../Numbers/Seven]]` ## Embedding hint The weekly sign-covenant of rest — distinct as a covenant inscribed in time rather than place or person, oscillating with creation's heptadic rhythm.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'feeds' (freq 9) is peripheral; theme overlap: creation_new, spirit_breath.

from.title: Spirit Anointing Isaiah 61
from.body: # Spirit Anointing (Isaiah 61) ## The oracle "The Spirit of the Lord YHWH is upon me, because YHWH has anointed me to bring good news to the poor… to proclaim the year of YHWH's favor." ## Date / context Isaiah's restoration oracles, exilic-postexilic horizon. ## Fulfillment Jesus reads this scroll in the Nazareth synagogue and declares, "Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing" (Luke 4:21), inaugurating his messianic ministry. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Galilee]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint The Messiah's self-announced mission statement — Spirit, gospel, jubilee.

to.title: Time Times Half Time
to.body: # Time, Times, and Half a Time ## The oracle The persecution of the saints is limited to "a time, times, and half a time" — a fixed and bounded period of tribulation. ## Date / context Danielic visions, 6th c. BC. ## Fulfillment Revelation reuses the formula (12:14) alongside parallel measures (forty-two months, 1260 days) to describe the church's wilderness period under satanic assault. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint The bounded-tribulation motif — evil's permission has a fixed expiration.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'μηρός' (thigh); both units touch on kingdom, name, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Obadiah Day Of The LORD
from.body: # Obadiah / Day of the LORD on Edom → Final Judgment ## OT Story — Obadiah 1:15-21 'The day of the LORD is near upon all the nations… As you have done, it shall be done to you.' The kingdom will be the LORD's. ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 12:19: Vengeance is mine, says the Lord. - Revelation 19:11-21: The rider judges the nations. - Hebrews 12:23: Mount Zion's assembly. ## Shared structure - Justice in measure of the deed. - Day of the LORD as judgment-day. - Saviors on Mount Zion. - Kingdom belongs to the LORD. ## Theological motif Eschatological day-of-the-LORD judgment. ## Counterarguments 1. **Obadiah is short and local.** *Its theological grammar of measured justice is fully integrated into NT eschatology.* 2. **Edom is specific.** *Edom functions as paradigm of pride-against-Zion.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about the day of the LORD, just retribution, or kingdom belonging to the LORD.

to.title: King
to.body: # King ## What this office is The Israelite king ruled as YHWH's vice-regent, charged with justice, defending the poor, leading in covenant fidelity, and shepherding the people. The Davidic covenant promised an eternal dynasty from David's line. The king was anointed (mashiach) with oil to mark divine appointment. ## Typological significance / Christ as fulfillment Jesus, son of David, is the promised eternal King whose throne endures forever. Where Israel's kings repeatedly failed in justice and idolatry, Christ reigns in perfect righteousness — King of kings and Lord of lords. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/David]]`, `[[../Persons/Solomon]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]`, `[[../Places/Mount-Zion]]`, `[[../Places/Jerusalem]]` ## Embedding hint Office of anointed royal rule as God's vice-regent over His people.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'κτίσμα' (creature) appears in both 'Revelation 5 Slain Lamb Worthy' and 'Revelation 8 Trumpets' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Revelation 5 Slain Lamb Worthy
from.body: # Revelation 5: Slain Lamb Worthy ## NT Text — Rev 5:6-14 Between the throne and the elders stood a Lamb as though slain. The Lion of Judah is also the slain-yet-standing Lamb. He alone is worthy to open the scroll. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 49:9-10** — Lion of Judah. - **Exod 12** — Passover lamb. - **Isa 53:7** — Lamb led to slaughter. ## Shared structure - Lamb-as-slain - Standing posture - Seven horns and eyes - Worthy declaration - New song ## Theological motif Lamb-Of-God enthroned; victory by sacrificial slaughter, not domination. ## Counterarguments 1. **Lion-Lamb tension?** *Resolved: lion conquers as slain lamb.* 2. **Why 'as though slain'?** *Eternally bearing the marks of his self-offering.* ## Embedding hint A slain-lamb worthy vision fusing Genesis 49's lion with Exodus/Isaiah's lamb.

to.title: Revelation 8 Trumpets
to.body: # Revelation 8: Seven Trumpets ## NT Text — Rev 8:6-13 Seven trumpets sound. Hail and fire mixed with blood, sea turned to blood, water made bitter — Egyptian plagues recapitulated against the world. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 7-12** — Egyptian plagues. - **Joel 2:1-11** — Trumpet alarm and locust army. - **Josh 6** — Jericho trumpets. ## Shared structure - Trumpet-summons - Cosmic plagues - Egypt-echo - Climactic judgment ## Theological motif Exodus plagues universalized; Joel's locust-army and Jericho-trumpets backdrop. ## Counterarguments 1. **Literal plagues?** *Symbol-thick imagery with real referents.* 2. **Comprehensive judgment?** *Successively more severe.* ## Embedding hint A seven-trumpet judgment recapitulating Egyptian plagues and Joel's day-of-the-Lord.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'κιθάρα' (harp) appears in both 'Altar Of Incense Prayer' and 'Daniel Sealed Book' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Altar Of Incense Prayer
from.body: # Altar of Incense → Prayers of the Saints ## OT Story — Exodus 30:1-10 Before the veil stands the golden altar of incense, kindled morning and evening with fragrant smoke perpetually before the LORD. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 5:8: Golden bowls of incense, which are the prayers of the saints. - Revelation 8:3-4: Incense and prayers rise together before God. - Luke 1:10-13: Zechariah at the incense altar receives Gabriel's announcement. ## Shared structure - Continuous fragrant ascent before God. - Position closest to the veil — boundary of presence. - Prayer as priestly act. - Mediator's hand offers incense / Christ intercedes. ## Theological motif Prayer as incense — mediated by the priest-king. ## Counterarguments 1. **Incense is purely cultic.** *Rev 5:8 explicitly identifies it with the prayers of the saints.* 2. **Zechariah's vision is incidental setting.** *Luke deliberately situates the gospel's start at the altar.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about incense, prayers rising, or the priest at the altar of fragrance.

to.title: Daniel Sealed Book
to.body: # Sealed Book of Daniel → Lamb Opens the Scroll ## OT Story — Daniel 12:4, 9 'Seal the book until the time of the end.' The vision is closed and reserved. ## NT Fulfillment - Revelation 5:1-9: Who is worthy to open the scroll? The Lion-Lamb. - Revelation 22:10: Do not seal up the words — the time is near. - 1 Peter 1:10-12: The prophets searched what time the Spirit indicated. ## Shared structure - Sealed book until the appointed time. - Opening requires worthy mediator. - Revelation reverses the seal in Christ. - Gospel reveals what prophets sought. ## Theological motif Lamb opens what Daniel sealed. ## Counterarguments 1. **Sealing is contextual to Daniel's day.** *Revelation makes it canon-spanning.* 2. **Scroll-imagery is generic.** *Its specific seal/unseal dynamic is uniquely Daniel-Revelation.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a sealed scroll, opened by a worthy one, or revealing what was hidden.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'δουλεία' (of bondage) appears in both 'Hebrews 2 Tasted Death For Everyone' and 'Romans 8 No Condemnation' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Hebrews 2 Tasted Death For Everyone
from.body: # Hebrews 2: Tasted Death for Everyone ## NT Text — Heb 2:9-18 Jesus, made lower than angels for a little while, tasted death for everyone. He is a merciful and faithful high priest because he shared in our flesh. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 8** — Made lower than the heavenly beings. - **Isa 53:11** — Bear iniquities. - **Job 19:25** — Redeemer who stands. ## Shared structure - Incarnation as solidarity - Death tasted - Priestly mercy - Brothers shared ## Theological motif Kinsman-Redeemer logic: only one who shares flesh-and-blood can redeem flesh-and-blood. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universal scope?** *Sufficient for all; effective for those sanctified.* 2. **Christ subordinated?** *Voluntary subordination 'for a little while.'* ## Embedding hint An incarnation-as-solidarity argument framing Christ as flesh-sharing high priest.

to.title: Romans 8 No Condemnation
to.body: # Romans 8: No Condemnation ## NT Text — Rom 8:1-39 Paul declares no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus. The chapter weaves Spirit-life, adoption, suffering, and unbreakable love. ## OT Antecedents - **Isa 50:8-9** — Who is my adversary? Let him come near. - **Ps 44:22** — For your sake we are killed all day long — quoted. - **Hos 11** — Father's love for prodigal son. ## Shared structure - Forensic verdict - Spirit indwelling - Adoption - Suffering and groaning - Unbreakable love ## Theological motif Eschatological verdict declared in present; all OT love-language gathered into Christ-love. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universalism implied?** *No — those in Christ; the qualifier matters.* 2. **Why so triumphant?** *The cross has settled the verdict.* ## Embedding hint A chapter-length declaration of no-condemnation and unbreakable love climaxing OT covenant-love.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'παράβασις' (transgression) appears in both 'Adam Christ Two Heads Romans 5' and 'Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Adam Christ Two Heads Romans 5
from.body: # Adam and Christ as Two Heads (Rom 5) ## NT Text — Rom 5:12-21 Paul reads humanity as represented by two federal heads: Adam, by whom sin and death entered, and Christ, by whom righteousness and life come. Two acts, two reigns, two destinies. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 2-3** — Adam's covenant headship and fall. - **Ps 51:5** — Inherited sinfulness. - **Hos 6:7** — Like Adam, they transgressed the covenant. ## Shared structure - Federal head - One act with cosmic effect - Sin/righteousness imputed - Death/life reign ## Theological motif Substitutionary-Sacrifice presupposes Adam-Christ headship; the second Adam undoes the first's catastrophe. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is federal headship fair?** *Same logic of representation that gives us blessing in Christ.* 2. **Is Romans 5 historical?** *Paul treats Adam as historical man with cosmic effect.* ## Embedding hint A federal-headship contrast between Adam and Christ governing the two ages of humanity.

to.title: Hebrews 9 Mediator Of New Covenant
to.body: # Hebrews 9: Mediator of the New Covenant ## NT Text — Heb 9:15-22 Christ is mediator of a new covenant. Where a covenant exists, the death of the covenant-maker must be established. Sinai's blood-sprinkling foreshadowed Calvary's. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 24:6-8** — Moses sprinkles blood on the people. - **Jer 31:33** — New covenant. - **Gen 15:9-21** — Covenant-cutting with Abraham. ## Shared structure - Covenant established by death - Blood-sprinkling - Sin-bearing - Eternal inheritance secured ## Theological motif Sinai-covenant inauguration prefigures the new; substitutionary-sacrifice covenant-blood is Christ's own. ## Counterarguments 1. **Diatheke = will or covenant?** *Pun deliberate — both senses are operative.* 2. **Why blood-mediation?** *Life-for-life logic established in Lev 17.* ## Embedding hint A new-covenant mediation argument grounding inauguration in covenant-cutting blood.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ὁμοίωμα' (a likeness) appears in both 'Philippians 2 Christ Hymn Kenosis' and 'Romans 6 Baptized Into Death' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Philippians 2 Christ Hymn Kenosis
from.body: # Philippians 2: Christ Hymn Kenosis ## NT Text — Phil 2:5-11 Christ, though in the form of God, emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross — and was exalted to receive every knee's bow. ## OT Antecedents - **Isa 45:23** — Every knee shall bow to me — quoted. - **Isa 52:13-53:12** — Servant abased and exalted. - **Dan 7:13-14** — Universal dominion given. ## Shared structure - Pre-existence in glory - Self-emptying - Servant form - Obedient unto death - Exaltation - Universal worship ## Theological motif Suffering-Servant arc fused with divine-name acclamation; Isa 45's God-only worship language applied to Jesus. ## Counterarguments 1. **What is emptied?** *Status-use, not divine nature.* 2. **Is this a hymn?** *Likely a pre-Pauline confession Paul incorporates.* ## Embedding hint A kenosis-and-exaltation hymn fusing Isaiah 45's divine worship with the Servant's abasement-glory arc.

to.title: Romans 6 Baptized Into Death
to.body: # Romans 6: Baptized into His Death ## NT Text — Rom 6:1-11 Believers are baptized into Christ's death and raised to walk in newness of life. Baptism is union with the Death-and-Resurrection of Christ. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 14** — Red Sea passage as death/resurrection of Israel. - **Josh 3** — Jordan crossing into the land. - **Ezek 36:25-27** — Sprinkled with clean water; new spirit. ## Shared structure - Going under water - Union with death - Coming up - Newness of life ## Theological motif Exodus-water-passage typology informs baptismal theology: passing through death to inheritance. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is union just symbolic?** *Real union mediated by Spirit and sealed in baptism.* 2. **Why repeat baptismal language?** *Paul grounds ethics in identity.* ## Embedding hint A baptismal participation in death-and-resurrection grounded in exodus-water typology.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'Salmon' (Σαλμών); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and seed worth surfacing.

from.title: Boaz Ruth
from.body: # Boaz and Ruth → Christ the Kinsman-Redeemer The book of Ruth is structured as an extended illustration of the *goel* (kinsman-redeemer) law in Leviticus 25. It is also the only OT book named after a Gentile, foreshadowing the inclusion of the nations in Christ. ## OT Story — Ruth 2-4 Naomi loses her husband and both sons in Moab. She returns to Bethlehem as a widow, with her **Moabite (Gentile)** daughter-in-law Ruth, who refuses to leave her: *"Your people will be my people, and your God my God."* In Bethlehem, Ruth gleans in the fields of **Boaz**, a wealthy kinsman of Naomi's deceased husband. Boaz: - Notices Ruth and shows her favor - Protects her from harm - Provides for her abundantly (more than the gleaning law required) - Is identified as one of Naomi's *go'alim* (potential kinsman-redeemers) Naomi instructs Ruth to approach Boaz at the threshing floor at night and "uncover his feet" — the customary appeal for redemption. Boaz responds: > *"And now, my daughter, do not fear. I will do for you all that you ask, for all my fellow townsmen know that you are a worthy woman."* (Ruth 3:11) But there is a **nearer kinsman** with first right of redemption. Boaz approaches him at the city gate. The nearer kinsman is willing to redeem Naomi's land — but unwilling to take Ruth, *"lest I impair my own inheritance"* (Ruth 4:6). He defers to Boaz. Boaz redeems both the land and Ruth. They marry. Ruth bears Obed, who becomes **the grandfather of King David** — and therefore an anc

to.title: Ruth Genealogy Of David
to.body: # Ruth in David's Line → Gentile in Christ's Line ## OT Story — Ruth 4:18-22 The book closes with a genealogy: Perez to David, through Boaz and Ruth. The Moabite outsider becomes great-grandmother of the king. ## NT Fulfillment - Matthew 1:5-6: Ruth named in Christ's genealogy. - Galatians 3:8: Scripture foresaw God would justify the Gentiles. - Isaiah 56:6-8: Foreigners joined to the LORD. ## Shared structure - Outsider grafted into covenant line. - Moabite — exclusionary stigma overcome. - Bethlehem as birthplace of king and Messiah. - Genealogy as theological argument. ## Theological motif Gentile inclusion at the heart of David's line. ## Counterarguments 1. **Moabites are formally excluded (Deut 23).** *Ruth's individual faith opens the boundary; canon shows mercy outpacing exclusion.* 2. **Genealogies are dry.** *Matthew's deliberate naming of four women (incl. Ruth) makes the genealogy a theological claim.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about Gentile in the messianic line, Moabite included, or genealogy of grace.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἐξαλείφω' (blotting out) appears in both 'Revelation 3 White Garments' and 'Revelation 7 Great Multitude' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Revelation 3 White Garments
from.body: # Revelation 3: White Garments ## NT Text — Rev 3:4-5 Those who have not soiled their garments will walk with me in white. Conqueror clothed in white — name not blotted from book of life. ## OT Antecedents - **Eccl 9:8** — Let your garments be always white. - **Isa 1:18** — White as snow. - **Zech 3:3-5** — Joshua's filthy garments exchanged for clean. ## Shared structure - Garment imagery - White as purity - Walking with the Lord - Book of life confession ## Theological motif Joshua-the-high-priest typology of cleansed garments applied to the saints. ## Counterarguments 1. **Self-effort?** *Worthiness is grace-given; garment is gift.* 2. **Universalism?** *No — qualifying language ('those who conquer').* ## Embedding hint A white-garments promise echoing Zechariah 3's cleansing of the priest.

to.title: Revelation 7 Great Multitude
to.body: # Revelation 7: Great Multitude ## NT Text — Rev 7:9-17 A great multitude no one could number, from every nation, tribe, people, language, standing before the throne and the Lamb. Abrahamic promise fulfilled. ## OT Antecedents - **Gen 12:3** — All families blessed. - **Gen 22:17** — Stars and sand-multitude. - **Isa 49:6** — Light to the nations. ## Shared structure - Innumerable multitude - Diverse origin - White robes - Palm branches - Lamb-as-shepherd ## Theological motif Abrahamic promise of innumerable seed fulfilled in the gathered church. ## Counterarguments 1. **Different from 144k?** *Same group, two views: faithful Israel and worldwide harvest.* 2. **Universalism?** *Conditioned: those washed in the blood of the Lamb.* ## Embedding hint An innumerable nations-multitude fulfilling Abrahamic seed-promise before the Lamb.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'mortal' (freq 6) is peripheral; theme overlap: creation_new, resurrection.

from.title: Isaiah 25 Banquet On Mountain
from.body: # Banquet on the Mountain / Death Swallowed Up → Marriage Supper ## OT Story — Isaiah 25:6-9 On this mountain the LORD will make a feast of rich food for all peoples; he will swallow up death forever and wipe away tears. ## NT Fulfillment - Matthew 8:11: Many will come from east and west to recline with Abraham. - Revelation 19:9: Blessed are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb. - 1 Corinthians 15:54: Death is swallowed up in victory. ## Shared structure - Mountain-feast for all nations. - Death swallowed (cosmic reversal). - Tears wiped away. - Eschatological banquet pattern. ## Theological motif Eschatological banquet — death undone. ## Counterarguments 1. **Israel-only feast.** *'All peoples' is in the very text; canon expands explicitly.* 2. **Tears-wiping is Revelation's image.** *Revelation deliberately echoes Isaiah 25.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a banquet on the mountain, death swallowed, or tears wiped away.

to.title: Romans 8 No Condemnation
to.body: # Romans 8: No Condemnation ## NT Text — Rom 8:1-39 Paul declares no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus. The chapter weaves Spirit-life, adoption, suffering, and unbreakable love. ## OT Antecedents - **Isa 50:8-9** — Who is my adversary? Let him come near. - **Ps 44:22** — For your sake we are killed all day long — quoted. - **Hos 11** — Father's love for prodigal son. ## Shared structure - Forensic verdict - Spirit indwelling - Adoption - Suffering and groaning - Unbreakable love ## Theological motif Eschatological verdict declared in present; all OT love-language gathered into Christ-love. ## Counterarguments 1. **Universalism implied?** *No — those in Christ; the qualifier matters.* 2. **Why so triumphant?** *The cross has settled the verdict.* ## Embedding hint A chapter-length declaration of no-condemnation and unbreakable love climaxing OT covenant-love.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἐφάπαξ' (once for all) appears in both 'Romans 6 Baptized Into Death' and 'Two Goats Yom Kippur' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Romans 6 Baptized Into Death
from.body: # Romans 6: Baptized into His Death ## NT Text — Rom 6:1-11 Believers are baptized into Christ's death and raised to walk in newness of life. Baptism is union with the Death-and-Resurrection of Christ. ## OT Antecedents - **Exod 14** — Red Sea passage as death/resurrection of Israel. - **Josh 3** — Jordan crossing into the land. - **Ezek 36:25-27** — Sprinkled with clean water; new spirit. ## Shared structure - Going under water - Union with death - Coming up - Newness of life ## Theological motif Exodus-water-passage typology informs baptismal theology: passing through death to inheritance. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is union just symbolic?** *Real union mediated by Spirit and sealed in baptism.* 2. **Why repeat baptismal language?** *Paul grounds ethics in identity.* ## Embedding hint A baptismal participation in death-and-resurrection grounded in exodus-water typology.

to.title: Two Goats Yom Kippur
to.body: # Two Goats of Atonement → Christ's Death and Bearing-Away ## OT Story — Leviticus 16:7-22 On Yom Kippur, two goats: one slaughtered as sin offering, its blood taken behind the veil; the other (the scapegoat) bears confessed sins into the wilderness, never to return. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 9:11-14: Christ entered the holy places by his own blood. - Hebrews 10:11-14: One offering for sins forever. - John 1:29: The Lamb of God who takes away (bears away) the sin of the world. ## Shared structure - Two complementary aspects of one atonement. - Blood applied / sin removed. - Wilderness as place of expulsion. - Annual repetition replaced by once-for-all. ## Theological motif Substitution and removal in one event at the cross. ## Counterarguments 1. **Two goats = two distinct theologies.** *They are commanded as a unity, fulfilled in one person.* 2. **Azazel-goat may be a demon-tribute.** *Even if so, the NT redirects: sin sent back where it came from.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about two goats, blood within / sins removed, or Christ bearing away sin.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'הֲדֹם' (the.footstool) appears in both 'Peters Sermon Joel Fulfilled' and 'Stephen Sees Son Of Man' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Peters Sermon Joel Fulfilled
from.body: # Peter's Sermon: Joel Fulfilled ## NT Text — Acts 2:14-41 Peter interprets Pentecost via Joel 2 and Psalms 16 and 110. The resurrection-and-ascension is read through the OT he learned to read on Emmaus. ## OT Antecedents - **Joel 2:28-32** — Pour out my Spirit. - **Ps 16:8-11** — You will not abandon to Sheol. - **Ps 110:1** — Sit at my right hand. ## Shared structure - OT-fulfillment exposition - Resurrection center - Call to repent - Promise to all who are far off ## Theological motif Apostolic preaching = Christological reading of OT. ## Counterarguments 1. **Is Peter's hermeneutic legitimate?** *It is Jesus' Emmaus method enacted.* 2. **Why these texts?** *They cover Spirit, resurrection, and enthronement.* ## Embedding hint An apostolic sermon reading Joel 2 and Psalms 16, 110 as fulfilled in the risen Christ.

to.title: Stephen Sees Son Of Man
to.body: # Stephen Sees the Son of Man Standing ## NT Text — Acts 7:55-56 Stephen sees the Son of Man standing at God's right hand. The Sanhedrin had condemned Jesus for the same vision; now the vision is publicly confirmed. ## OT Antecedents - **Dan 7:13-14** — Son of Man comes to Ancient of Days. - **Ps 110:1** — Sit at my right hand. - **Ezek 1:26** — One like a son of man on the throne. ## Shared structure - Heavenly vision - Son-of-Man title - Right-hand position - Standing posture (witness/welcome) ## Theological motif Daniel-7 vision confirmed at the moment of the first martyr's death. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why standing not sitting?** *Either welcoming Stephen or judicial witness.* 2. **Self-fulfilling vision?** *Vision-form is canonical from Daniel.* ## Embedding hint A martyr's heavenly vision confirming the Daniel-7 Son-of-Man enthronement.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'διανοίγω' (Be opened) appears in both 'Stephen Sees Son Of Man' and 'Stephens Martyrdom Like Christ' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Stephen Sees Son Of Man
from.body: # Stephen Sees the Son of Man Standing ## NT Text — Acts 7:55-56 Stephen sees the Son of Man standing at God's right hand. The Sanhedrin had condemned Jesus for the same vision; now the vision is publicly confirmed. ## OT Antecedents - **Dan 7:13-14** — Son of Man comes to Ancient of Days. - **Ps 110:1** — Sit at my right hand. - **Ezek 1:26** — One like a son of man on the throne. ## Shared structure - Heavenly vision - Son-of-Man title - Right-hand position - Standing posture (witness/welcome) ## Theological motif Daniel-7 vision confirmed at the moment of the first martyr's death. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why standing not sitting?** *Either welcoming Stephen or judicial witness.* 2. **Self-fulfilling vision?** *Vision-form is canonical from Daniel.* ## Embedding hint A martyr's heavenly vision confirming the Daniel-7 Son-of-Man enthronement.

to.title: Stephens Martyrdom Like Christ
to.body: # Stephen's Martyrdom Patterned on Christ ## NT Text — Acts 7:54-60 Stephen prays 'receive my spirit' and 'do not hold this sin against them' — directly echoing the cross-words of Jesus.
