Slain, then raised after three and a half days. ## OT Antecedents - **1 Kgs 17:1** — Elijah shuts up the heavens. - **Exod 7:17-20** — Moses turns water to blood. - **Zech 4** — Two olive trees and lampstands. ## Shared structure - Two witnesses - Sackcloth prophecy - Elijah-Moses signs - Death and resurrection - Vindication ## Theological motif Elijah-Moses typology continues in church's two-pronged witness; resurrection of the witnesses prefigures the saints. ## Counterarguments 1. **Identifiable individuals?** *Pattern more than persons; cessation impossible.* 2. **Three-and-a-half days?** *Limited tribulation, dependent on Daniel's 'time, times, half a time.'* ## Embedding hint A two-witnesses ministry-and-resurrection echoing Elijah-Moses signs and Zechariah 4.

to.title: Time Times Half Time
to.body: # Time, Times, and Half a Time ## The oracle The persecution of the saints is limited to "a time, times, and half a time" — a fixed and bounded period of tribulation. ## Date / context Danielic visions, 6th c. BC. ## Fulfillment Revelation reuses the formula (12:14) alongside parallel measures (forty-two months, 1260 days) to describe the church's wilderness period under satanic assault. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint The bounded-tribulation motif — evil's permission has a fixed expiration.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared rare term 'μέτωπον' (foreheads) links 'Revelation 14 Lamb On Zion' and 'Revelation 20 Millennium' through creation, kingship.

from.title: Revelation 14 Lamb On Zion
from.body: # Revelation 14: Lamb on Mount Zion ## NT Text — Rev 14:1-5 The Lamb stands on Mount Zion with 144,000 bearing his Father's name on their foreheads. Psalm 2's enthronement on Zion fulfilled. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 2:6** — I have set my king on Zion, my holy hill. - **Joel 2:32** — Mount Zion shall be refuge. - **Obad 17** — On Mount Zion there shall be deliverance. ## Shared structure - Lamb enthroned - Mount Zion - Sealed remnant - New song - Firstfruits ## Theological motif Zion fulfilled in the Lamb-king and his redeemed; counter-Zion to Babylon. ## Counterarguments 1. **Earthly Zion?** *Heavenly antitype of which earthly is shadow.* 2. **Celibate elites?** *'Virginal' is symbolic purity, not literal celibacy.* ## Embedding hint A Lamb-on-Zion enthronement of the sealed firstfruits-multitude.

to.title: Revelation 20 Millennium
to.body: # Revelation 20: The Millennium ## NT Text — Rev 20:1-6 The dragon is bound; the saints reign with Christ a thousand years. A debated vision of the church-age or future kingdom. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 90:4** — Thousand years as one day. - **Isa 65:18-25** — Long-lived peaceful kingdom. - **Ezek 37:24-28** — David shepherd-king of restored Israel. ## Shared structure - Dragon bound - First resurrection - Saints reign - Release for final rebellion - Final judgment ## Theological motif Kingdom-promise inaugurated; final defeat of evil deferred but certain. ## Counterarguments 1. **Premil/amil/postmil?** *All readings acknowledge the symbolic-historical interplay.* 2. **Two resurrections?** *First/second pattern is exegetically debated.* ## Embedding hint A thousand-year reign vision with binding-of-dragon and saints' enthronement.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἱματισμός' (clothing) appears in both 'Casting Lots For Garments' and 'Transfiguration Voice' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Casting Lots For Garments
from.body: # Casting Lots for the Garments ## NT Text — John 19:23-24 Soldiers divide Jesus' clothes and cast lots for his seamless tunic, fulfilling Psalm 22:18 and evoking the high priest's robe. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 22:18** — Divide my garments. - **Exod 28:31-32** — High-priestly robe without seam. - **Lev 21:10** — Priest must not tear his garments. ## Shared structure - Garments divided - Lot cast - Seamless tunic preserved - Priestly echo ## Theological motif Dying Messiah is also dying high priest; the seamless robe survives intact. ## Counterarguments 1. **Priestly link from John?** *John highlights it; the detail is gratuitous otherwise.* 2. **Coincidence?** *Pattern overwhelming.* ## Embedding hint Psalm 22's garment-division fulfilled with priestly typology in the seamless tunic.

to.title: Transfiguration Voice
to.body: # Transfiguration ## The appearance On a high mountain Jesus is transfigured before Peter, James, and John — his face like the sun, clothes white as light; Moses and Elijah appear; a bright cloud overshadows, and the voice says, "This is my beloved Son; listen to him." ## Mode / context Roughly six days after Caesarea Philippi, c. AD 29. ## Significance The Sinai-Horeb theophanies fulfilled in Christ's body; the law and prophets witness; the new Prophet-like-Moses is to be heeded. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Persons/Elijah]]`, `[[../Places/Galilee]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint Glory radiating from inside the man Jesus — theophany of the incarnate Son.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ὀργίζω' (being angry with) appears in both 'Great Banquet' and 'Unforgiving Servant' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Great Banquet
from.body: # The Great Banquet ## What happens A man holds a great banquet; the invited make excuses, so servants are sent into streets, lanes, highways and hedges to compel the poor, lame, blind, and Gentiles to come in. ## Typological significance Lukan counterpart to the wedding banquet: refusal by the privileged opens the table to outcasts and Gentiles. Kingdom-table populated by the unlikely. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Refused banquet filled from the lanes and hedges — kingdom for the outcast.

to.title: Unforgiving Servant
to.body: # The Unforgiving Servant ## What happens A king forgives a servant's incalculable debt; that servant then refuses to forgive a fellow-servant's small debt and is delivered to the tormentors. ## Typological significance Vertical mercy must flow horizontally or it is revoked — the disproportion of God's forgiveness obliges proportionate mercy to others. Frames the Lord's Prayer's 'as we forgive.' ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Ten-thousand-talent debt forgiven, denarius debt withheld — mercy revoked.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'διασκορπίζω' (you did scatter) appears in both 'Shrewd Manager' and 'Talents' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Shrewd Manager
from.body: # The Shrewd Manager ## What happens A dishonest steward, about to be dismissed, reduces his master's debtors' bills to secure future welcome. The master commends his shrewdness. ## Typological significance Use of unrighteous mammon to make friends for the age to come — the children of light are urged to learn strategic generosity from the world's strategists. Wealth as instrument, not master. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Shrewd use of mammon for eternal welcome — strategic generosity.

to.title: Talents
to.body: # The Talents ## What happens A master entrusts servants with five, two, and one talents. The first two double their trust; the third buries his and is condemned. ## Typological significance Stewardship parable of the interim: the kingdom delays so that fidelity may be tested. Risk-taking trust is the proper response; fear-driven preservation is judged as wickedness. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Entrusted talents multiplied by faith, lost by fear.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'γεμίζω' (is being filled up) appears in both 'Calming Of Storm' and 'Water Into Wine' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Calming Of Storm
from.body: # Calming of the Storm ## What happens Crossing the Sea of Galilee, Jesus sleeps in the stern through a violent storm; rebuked, the wind and sea fall silent. The disciples ask, 'Who then is this?' ## Typological significance Only YHWH stills the sea in the Psalms — Jesus' authority over chaos discloses His divine identity. The sleep-and-rise of the Lord in the boat patterns His death and resurrection over the chaos of the abyss. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Galilee]]` ## Embedding hint Sleeping Lord rises to silence the deep — Psalm 107 enacted in person.

to.title: Water Into Wine
to.body: # Water Into Wine ## What happens At a wedding in Cana, the wine runs out; Jesus transforms six stone jars of purification water into the best wine of the feast. ## Typological significance The first sign in John — purification water becomes wedding wine, signaling that the Law's cleansing rituals are fulfilled in the messianic banquet. The shift from six to abundant new wine inaugurates the new covenant. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Mary-Mother-Of-Jesus]]`, `[[../Places/Galilee]]` ## Embedding hint Purification water becomes wedding wine — the messianic feast inaugurated.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'שִׁתִּין' (sixty) appears in both 'Daniel Lions Den' and 'Three In Fiery Furnace' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Daniel Lions Den
from.body: # Daniel in the Lions' Den ## What happens Sealed in a den of lions for praying to YHWH, Daniel emerges unharmed at dawn; the king proclaims YHWH the living God. ## Typological significance Sealed in a stone pit at evening, emerging alive at dawn — a transparent resurrection pattern. The sealed stone, the night vigil, the dawn vindication all anticipate the empty tomb. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs/Death-And-Resurrection]]`, `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint Sealed pit, dawn vindication — exilic resurrection prefigured.

to.title: Three In Fiery Furnace
to.body: # Three in the Fiery Furnace ## What happens Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, thrown into a furnace heated sevenfold, walk unharmed with a fourth figure 'like a son of the gods.' ## Typological significance Companioned through fire by a divine presence — pre-incarnate Christ walking with His own through judgment. The fire burns only the bonds. ## Connects to `[[../Numbers]]` (Seven, Three) ## Embedding hint Three faithful and a fourth in the fire — Christ with His own through judgment.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'λέων' (of the lion) appears in both 'Daniel Lions Den' and 'Shiloh Of Judah' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Daniel Lions Den
from.body: # Daniel in the Lions' Den ## What happens Sealed in a den of lions for praying to YHWH, Daniel emerges unharmed at dawn; the king proclaims YHWH the living God. ## Typological significance Sealed in a stone pit at evening, emerging alive at dawn — a transparent resurrection pattern. The sealed stone, the night vigil, the dawn vindication all anticipate the empty tomb. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs/Death-And-Resurrection]]`, `[[../Persons/Daniel]]` ## Embedding hint Sealed pit, dawn vindication — exilic resurrection prefigured.

to.title: Shiloh Of Judah
to.body: # Shiloh of Judah ## The oracle Jacob, blessing his sons, declares that the scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor a ruler's staff from between his feet, until Shiloh come, and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples. ## Date / context Patriarchal blessing in Egypt at the end of Genesis, c. 1800 BC. ## Fulfillment Fulfilled in Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5), who is born of Judah (Hebrews 7:14) and to whom the nations bring obedience. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Jacob]]`, `[[../Titles/Messiah]]` ## Embedding hint First explicit narrowing of the messianic line to the tribe of Judah and the gathering of the peoples.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'ἀναγκάζω' (He compelled) appears in both 'Walking On Water' and 'Great Banquet' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Walking On Water
from.body: # Walking on Water ## What happens After the feeding, Jesus walks on the storm-tossed sea in the fourth watch and says 'It is I; do not fear' — and the wind ceases when He enters the boat. ## Typological significance Treading on the sea is YHWH's prerogative in Job and the Psalms; Jesus' 'I AM' to His disciples is a quiet theophany. He passes by them as YHWH passed by Moses on Sinai. ## Connects to `[[../Places/Galilee]]` ## Embedding hint YHWH's sea-tread enacted; 'I AM' spoken on the waves.

to.title: Great Banquet
to.body: # The Great Banquet ## What happens A man holds a great banquet; the invited make excuses, so servants are sent into streets, lanes, highways and hedges to compel the poor, lame, blind, and Gentiles to come in. ## Typological significance Lukan counterpart to the wedding banquet: refusal by the privileged opens the table to outcasts and Gentiles. Kingdom-table populated by the unlikely. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Refused banquet filled from the lanes and hedges — kingdom for the outcast.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'πνέω' (blew) appears in both 'Ezekiel 36 New Heart' and 'Revelation 7 144000 Sealed' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Ezekiel 36 New Heart
from.body: # Ezekiel 36 / New Heart, New Spirit → Born of Water and Spirit ## OT Story — Ezekiel 36:25-27 'I will sprinkle clean water on you… A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you. I will remove the heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh.' ## NT Fulfillment - John 3:5-8: Born of water and Spirit. - Titus 3:5: Washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit. - 2 Corinthians 3:3: Tablets of human hearts. ## Shared structure - Clean water of purification. - Stone heart replaced by heart of flesh. - Spirit indwelling. - Walk in God's statutes empowered. ## Theological motif Regeneration in the new covenant. ## Counterarguments 1. **Water in Ezekiel is ceremonial.** *John 3 makes the canonical move to spiritual cleansing.* 2. **Heart-language is metaphor.** *Yes, and the metaphor names the deepest reality.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about clean water, heart of flesh replacing stone, or Spirit put within.

to.title: Revelation 7 144000 Sealed
to.body: # Revelation 7: 144,000 Sealed ## NT Text — Rev 7:1-8 A great seal placed on the foreheads of 144,000 from all the tribes of Israel. Ezekiel's marking of the faithful is echoed. ## OT Antecedents - **Ezek 9:4-6** — Mark on the foreheads of those who sigh. - **Num 1** — Tribal census. - **Isa 49:6** — Restoration of preserved Israel. ## Shared structure - Mark on foreheads - Tribal listing - Preservation amid judgment - Twelve-thousand each ## Theological motif Ezekiel-9 preservation-mark applied to the messianic Israel. ## Counterarguments 1. **Literal 144k?** *Symbolic completeness — twelve squared times thousand.* 2. **Ethnic Israel only?** *Symbolic Israel including the church.* ## Embedding hint A foreheads-sealed remnant preservation echoing Ezekiel 9's mark.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: shared word 'two-edged' (freq 3) is peripheral; theme overlap: kingship, judgment.

from.title: Josiahs Reforms
from.body: # Josiah Finds the Book / Reform → Word-Rediscovered Renewal ## OT Story — 2 Kings 22-23 During temple repairs, the Book of the Law is found. Josiah hears it read, tears his clothes, and leads sweeping reform: idols destroyed, Passover restored. ## NT Fulfillment - Hebrews 4:12: The word of God is living and active. - Acts 17:30-31: God commands all people everywhere to repent. - Ezra 8-Nehemiah 8: Scripture-driven renewal repeated. ## Shared structure - Word rediscovered triggers repentance. - Reform from the top down. - Idols destroyed; covenant renewed. - Passover restored at the center. ## Theological motif Word of God as engine of renewal. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why was the Book lost?** *The narrative's diagnosis: neglect under wicked kings. The retrieval is grace.* 2. **Josiah's reform didn't last.** *No — but the pattern of word-driven renewal is canonical (Acts 2; Reformation).* ## Embedding hint Match passages about scripture rediscovered, reform driven by the word, or torn garments at hearing the word.

to.title: Daniel Ancient Of Days Vision
to.body: # Daniel's Ancient of Days Vision ## The appearance Daniel sees thrones placed, the Ancient of Days take his seat — clothing white as snow, hair like pure wool, throne flaming, wheels of fire; a stream of fire issues from before him; one like a son of man comes to him and is given dominion. ## Mode / context First-year-of-Belshazzar visions, c. 553 BC. ## Significance The fullest OT picture of divine judicial court convening; pairs the Ancient of Days with the Son of Man. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Daniel]]`, `[[../Titles/Son-Of-Man]]` ## Embedding hint The bench-of-judgment theophany — fire, white wool, books, and an investiture.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo on 'beautiful' (ὡραῖος); meaningful thematic overlap between seed and seed worth surfacing.

from.title: Healing At Beautiful Gate
from.body: # Healing at the Beautiful Gate ## NT Text — Acts 3:1-10 Peter heals a lame man at the temple gate. The healing fulfills Isaiah 35:6 ('the lame shall leap') and enacts kingdom restoration at the temple's threshold. ## OT Antecedents - **Isa 35:6** — Then shall the lame leap like a deer. - **Mic 4:6-7** — I will assemble the lame. - **2 Sam 5:8** — David's saying about the lame. ## Shared structure - Liminal location - Lame restored - Public sign - Messianic-age marker ## Theological motif Isaiah-35 jubilation enacted; the kingdom touches Israel at temple's gate. ## Counterarguments 1. **Common healing only?** *Acts uses it to launch sermon on resurrection.* 2. **Why Beautiful Gate?** *Liminal threshold of restoration.* ## Embedding hint A messianic-age healing at the temple gate fulfilling Isaiah 35.

to.title: Nahum Good News On Mountains
to.body: # Beautiful Feet of the Good-News-Bringer → Gospel Heralds ## OT Story — Nahum 1:15; Isaiah 52:7 'How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace, who says to Zion, Your God reigns!' ## NT Fulfillment - Romans 10:15: How beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news. - Ephesians 6:15: Feet shod with the readiness given by the gospel of peace. - Acts 10:36: The good news of peace through Jesus Christ. ## Shared structure - Herald with good news of peace. - Mountain-top proclamation. - 'Your God reigns' as kingdom-claim. - Feet of evangelists / apostolic mission. ## Theological motif Heralds of the kingdom — feet on mountains. ## Counterarguments 1. **Nahum is about Nineveh's fall.** *Yes — the good news for Judah is its corollary; Paul universalizes.* 2. **Imagery is poetic.** *Paul makes it grammar of the gospel mission.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about beautiful feet on the mountains, gospel-heralding, or 'your God reigns.'

prior_rationale: Thin link: 'ὅσιος' (Holy One) is shared but only weakly relevant on at least one side.

from.title: Hebrews 7 Forever Priest
from.body: # Hebrews 7: A Priest Forever ## NT Text — Heb 7:23-28 Christ holds his priesthood permanently because he continues forever. He is able to save to the uttermost because he always lives to intercede. ## OT Antecedents - **Ps 110:4** — Priest forever. - **Lev 16** — Annual atonement — repeated. - **Num 25:13** — Phinehas's perpetual priesthood — partial type. ## Shared structure - Eternal priesthood - Permanent intercession - Holy, blameless, separated - Once-for-all sacrifice ## Theological motif Levitical priesthood's transience answered by Christ's permanence; substitutionary-sacrifice is final. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why not Levitical?** *Repetition signals incompleteness; Christ's once-for-all completes.* 2. **'Forever' how literal?** *As literal as 'priest after the order of Melchizedek' demands.* ## Embedding hint An eternal-priesthood argument grounding Christ's intercession in his indestructible life.

to.title: Psalm 16 Holy One Not Decay
to.body: # Psalm 16 / 'You Will Not Let Your Holy One See Decay' → Resurrection Predicted ## OT Story — Psalm 16 David sings: 'I have set the LORD always before me… you will not abandon my soul to Sheol or let your holy one see corruption. You will show me the path of life.' ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 2:25-32: Peter — David died and was buried; he prophesied Christ's resurrection. - Acts 13:35-37: Paul — the one God raised did not see corruption. - Romans 4:25: Raised for our justification. ## Shared structure - Confidence beyond death. - Holy One whose body does not decay. - Path of life beyond the grave. - David speaks of the greater Son. ## Theological motif Resurrection foretold in worship. ## Counterarguments 1. **David spoke of himself.** *Peter explicitly distinguishes: David died; his words apply to Christ.* 2. **Hebrew sheol-language is ambiguous.** *NT interprets canonically: not abandoned, not decayed.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about not seeing corruption, path of life from death, or the Holy One vindicated.

prior_rationale: Thin link: 'טָלֶה' (a.lamb) is shared but only weakly relevant on at least one side.

from.title: Isaiah 40 Voice Crying
from.body: # Voice in the Wilderness / Prepare the Way → John the Baptist ## OT Story — Isaiah 40:1-11 'Comfort, comfort my people. A voice cries: in the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD. Every valley shall be lifted up.' Glory revealed; all flesh sees together. ## NT Fulfillment - Mark 1:2-3: The opening of the gospel quotes this. - John 1:23: 'I am the voice…' — John's self-identification. - Luke 3:4-6: Salvation seen by all flesh. ## Shared structure - Voice / herald preceding the LORD. - Wilderness preparation. - Glory of the LORD revealed to all flesh. - Shepherd-arm gathering the lambs (v. 11). ## Theological motif Forerunner preparing the LORD's coming. ## Counterarguments 1. **Mark's citation conflates Mal 3:1 and Isa 40.** *The conflation is theological and tradition-rooted, not erroneous.* 2. **Original context is return from exile.** *Yes — and gospel writers see Christ as the greater return.* ## Embedding hint Match passages about a voice in the wilderness, preparing the way, or glory of the LORD revealed.

to.title: Revelation 21 New Jerusalem Descending
to.body: # Revelation 21: New Jerusalem Descending ## NT Text — Rev 21:1-22:5 John sees the new heaven and new earth and the New Jerusalem descending from heaven as a bride adorned. God dwells with men. ## OT Antecedents - **Isa 65:17-25** — New heavens and new earth. - **Ezek 40-48** — Vision of restored temple-city. - **Zech 8:3** — I will dwell in Zion. ## Shared structure - New cosmos - City descending - Bride-city - God-with-men - Twelve gates and foundations ## Theological motif Eden-Tabernacle-Temple-City-Bride imagery converges; God dwells with redeemed humanity. ## Counterarguments 1. **Why descend?** *Gift of God, not human achievement.* 2. **Earthly or heavenly?** *Heavenly origin descending into earthly fulfillment.* ## Embedding hint A New-Jerusalem-as-bride descent gathering Isaiah, Ezekiel, and Zechariah city-promises.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'טָלֶה' (a.lamb); both units touch on no clear thematic overlap, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

to.title: Wolf And Lamb
to.body: # Wolf and Lamb ## The oracle "The wolf shall dwell with the lamb… and a little child shall lead them. They shall not hurt or destroy in all my holy mountain." ## Date / context Isaiah's messianic-restoration vision. ## Fulfillment Awaits the consummation of the kingdom in the new creation; in the meantime read as the trajectory of peace under the Messiah's reign. ## Connects to `[[../Symbols/Lamb]]` ## Embedding hint Predator-prey reconciliation as image of creational peace under messianic rule.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo via 'χρίω' (He has anointed); both units touch on no clear thematic overlap, worth surfacing as hypothesis.

from.title: Psalm 2 Anointed King
from.body: # Psalm 2 / 'You Are My Son' → Messiah Enthroned Despite Nations' Rage ## OT Story — Psalm 2 Nations rage and kings plot against the LORD and his anointed. The LORD laughs; he installs his King on Zion. 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you. Ask of me, and I will give the nations as your inheritance.' ## NT Fulfillment - Acts 4:25-28: Quoted as fulfilled in the plot against Jesus. - Acts 13:33: 'You are my Son' applied to the resurrection. - Revelation 19:15: He will rule them with a rod of iron. ## Shared structure - Conspiracy of nations against the Anointed. - Sonship declared. - Inheritance of nations granted. - Iron-rod rule paired with refuge for those who trust. ## Theological motif Anointed Son enthroned. ## Counterarguments 1. **Royal psalm originally about Davidic king.** *Yes — and the NT reads its Messianic horizon as canonical.* 2. **Iron rod is harsh.** *The same psalm ends with 'Blessed are all who take refuge in him.'* ## Embedding hint Match passages about nations raging against the anointed, sonship declared, or inheritance of nations.

to.title: Sabbatical Year
to.body: # Sabbatical Year (Shemitah) ## What it is The seventh year in which the land lay fallow, Hebrew slaves were freed, and debts among Israelites were released, extending the Sabbath principle from week to year and from labor to economy. ## Key elements - Seven-year cycle, land rests in year seven - Spontaneous growth shared with poor and beasts - Release of debts among Israelites - Release of Hebrew bondservants (after six years) - Public reading of the law at Tabernacles every seventh year ## NT fulfillment Jesus inaugurates his ministry by reading Isaiah 61 "to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor" (Luke 4:18-19), claiming the sabbatical and jubilee patterns as fulfilled in himself — release for captives and good news for the poor. ## Connects to `[[../Festivals/Sabbath]]`, `[[../Festivals/Jubilee]]`, `[[../Covenants/Sabbath-Covenant]]`, `[[../Numbers/Seven]]` ## Embedding hint The seven-year land and debt release — uniquely an annual-scale Sabbath, semantically the agrarian-economic application of weekly rest.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'κλαυθμός' (weeping) appears in both 'Dragnet' and 'Wedding Banquet' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Dragnet
from.body: # The Dragnet ## What happens The kingdom is like a net cast into the sea that gathers fish of every kind; at shore the good are kept and the bad thrown away. ## Typological significance Maritime counterpart to the wheat-and-tares: mixed gathering now, sorting at the end. The fishermen-apostles' work foreshadowed as eschatological reach into all peoples. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Sea-wide net gathering all kinds — sorting deferred to shore.

to.title: Wedding Banquet
to.body: # The Wedding Banquet ## What happens A king prepares a wedding banquet for his son; the invited refuse, so servants are sent into the highways to gather all they find. One guest without a wedding garment is cast out. ## Typological significance Refusal of the original invitees clears the way for the streets — Gentile inclusion. Yet the wedding garment matters: grace is free but its proper response is reclothed righteousness. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint Banquet's invitees refusing, streets filling — but garments still required.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'περιβλέπω' (having looked around on) appears in both 'Healing Of Bleeding Woman' and 'Transfiguration' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Healing Of Bleeding Woman
from.body: # Healing of the Bleeding Woman ## What happens A woman with a twelve-year flow of blood touches the fringe of Jesus' garment in the crowd and is instantly healed; Jesus perceives that power has gone out from Him. ## Typological significance Levitical impurity reversed by contact with the Holy One — the contagion runs the wrong way. Twelve years parallels Jairus's daughter's age, framing daughters of Israel restored together. ## Connects to `[[../Numbers]]` (Twelve) ## Embedding hint Impurity flow halted by touching the fringe — holiness contagion reversed.

to.title: Transfiguration
to.body: # Transfiguration ## What happens On a high mountain, Jesus is transfigured before Peter, James, and John; His face shines, His garments become white as light, and Moses and Elijah appear with Him. ## Typological significance Mosaic Sinai theophany and Elijah's Horeb encounter converge on Jesus, who is shown to be the goal of Law and Prophets. The Father's voice — 'this is my beloved Son' — recapitulates the baptism. ## Connects to `[[../Persons/Moses]]`, `[[../Persons/Elijah]]` ## Embedding hint Law and Prophets converge in radiant flesh — Jesus revealed as the goal.

prior_rationale: Plausible echo: 'εἰ δὲ μή' (lest) appears in both 'Barren Fig Tree' and 'King Going To War' with meaningful thematic overlap.

from.title: Barren Fig Tree
from.body: # The Barren Fig Tree ## What happens A vineyard owner orders a barren fig tree cut down after three fruitless years; the vinedresser pleads for one more year of digging and dunging. ## Typological significance Patience under intercession — judgment delayed by mediator's plea. Three fruitless years parallel Jesus' public ministry; the cursed fig tree in Holy Week is this parable enacted. ## Connects to `[[../Numbers]]` (Three) ## Embedding hint Vinedresser's plea staying the axe one more year — patience under intercession.

to.title: King Going To War
to.body: # The King Going to War ## What happens What king goes to war with ten thousand against twenty thousand without first considering whether he can prevail? Otherwise he sues for peace while the other is far off. ## Typological significance Twin parable to the tower builder: discipleship is also strategic surrender. Either renounce everything in advance, or sue for peace before engagement. ## Connects to `[[../Motifs]]` ## Embedding hint King calculating odds before engagement — discipleship's prior surrender.
